US-Congo deal for cobalt supply and processing in Arizona
Published
–  May 21, 2026 03:37 pm BST
John
Read Later
US-DRC signing ceremony. Photo: EGC
The US and Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) have given the green light to set up a landmark responsible cobalt supply and processing chain.
DRC state cobalt producer Entreprise Générale du Cobalt (EGC) said on May 13 it had signed a memorandum of understanding with US firm EVelution Energy and global trading house, Trafigura, paving the way for launching a US commercial sulfate and cobalt metal processing project.
Construction work on the Arizona project, already under development by EVelution as the first such facility in the US, is scheduled to start next year with commissioning in 2029 ― subject to definitive agreements.
The memorandum follows the release of a damning new report from UN scientists, revealed by
Batteries International
, warning that mining critical minerals such as lithium and cobalt to power ‘clean energy’ systems is fuelling severe, hidden environmental and health crises.
However, EGC said the deal with the US would be key to strengthening secure, transparent, and sustainable supply chains for the US defence, aerospace, EV batteries, and advanced technology sectors.
EGC will ensure the origin and traceability of cobalt in the African nation, Trafigura will provide logistics and delivery services and EVelution will process the cobalt in Arizona.
A key element of the project will be the Lobito Atlantic Railway corridor ― connecting Kolwezi in the DRC to the port of Lobito in neighbouring Angola. The rail link was established with the support of international financing, including funding from the US, to improve the competitiveness and reliability of trade between Central Africa and international markets.
EGC chief executive Eric Kalala said: “The partnership demonstrates the DRC’s desire to integrate sustainably into the global value chains of critical minerals, while promoting local value creation, the transfer of skills and the development of a responsible sourcing model in line with international standards.”
EVelution announced on April 28 the signing of a binding, five-year offtake agreement with Mitsui & Co valued at around $850 million. Under the terms of the agreement, EVelution will supply a substantial majority (3,000 tonnes) of the cobalt metal production from its Arizona facility annually.
Research
published
by the US Geological Society in April this year said the country had estimated reserves of economically recoverable lithium to replace more than 300 years of imports at last year’s level, according to latest research.
Nevertheless, the US has been actively shoring up its supply chain of materials critical to the country’s energy security, including an
agreement
last year with Saudi Arabia.

title: Chinese scientists unveil prototype hydrogen BESS – Batteries International
content:
Advertisement
Home
News
News, Other Chemistries
Chinese scientists unveil prototype hydrogen BESS
Published
–  May 21, 2026 03:32 pm BST
John
Read Later
Ping Chen. Photo: DICP
Scientists in China have published details of a prototype gas-solid hydride ion battery they say stores electricity and hydrogen at the same time.
The team, led by scientists from the Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics (DICP) under the Chinese Academy of Sciences, published the findings of their research in the journal
Joule
on May 13.
Chinese state media agency Xinhua hailed the findings, noting that the new battery runs on hydride ions, instead of lithium or other common materials.
According to Xinhua, the team has been working on the project since 2018 — having first developed a new material in 2023 that allows hydride ions to move steadily at low temperatures. Two years later, they built the first all-solid-state hydride ion battery.
The prototype battery uses magnesium metal and hydrogen gas as its two electrodes. When the battery releases power, hydrogen gas turns into hydride ions, while the metal becomes a metal hydride. When the battery is charged, the process reverses, and the hydrogen can be released again. This means the battery stores both electricity and hydrogen at the same time.
According to the research team, the battery can work in temperatures ranging from -20°C to 90°C. Its initial discharge capacity reached 1,526 milliamp-hours per gram. It also maintained over 70% of its capacity after 60 charge-discharge cycles.
Team members said they stacked 10 small batteries together to form a larger battery pack, which produced more than 2.4V and successfully lit an LED bulb.
Ping Chen, a researcher at DICP, said: “The new battery achieved 93.9% energy efficiency, which is one-third higher than traditional thermal hydrogen storage methods.”
She said the new battery works at room temperature and normal pressure, storing hydrogen in the form of a solid metal hydride while the battery is being charged or discharged, eliminating the need for expensive high-pressure tanks or cryogenic cooling.

Samsung SDI president & CEO Yoon-ho Choi and GM chair and CEO Mary Barra
The future of a General Motors-Samsung SDI battery cell joint venture in the US is uncertain after the partners announced the project had been “paused”.
GM said last year that the $3.5 billion project in Indiana would launch in 2027 and produce NCA-based high-performance nickel-rich prismatic batteries for EV batteries.
However, a GM spokesperson told local media outlet ABC57 on May 14 that construction of the plant in New Carlisle was being paused “to align production capacity with current demand”.
GM said an update on plans for the site would be given at a later date.
Bill Schalliol, economic development director of St Joseph County in Indiana, told ABC57 the county was aware of a ‘pause condition’ activated at the project site and was working with GM, Indiana state and other partners.
When the project was announced in 2024, the partners said they would invest around $3.5 billion to build the plant, which would have an annual production capacity of 27GWh initially, targeting mass production in 2027. The annual capacity was expected to rise to 36GWh.

title: Ford gears up new $2bn BESS business to power US market – Batteries International
content:
Advertisement
Home

Energy Storage, Other Chemistries
Ford gears up new $2bn BESS business to power US market
Published
–  May 21, 2026 03:13 pm BST
John
Read Later
Auto giant Ford has formally entered the battery storage market with the launch of operations that are set to see at least 20GWh of capacity deployed annually.
Ford Energy, a wholly owned subsidiary of the Ford Motor Company, said on May 11 it expects to start BESS deliveries from late next year.
Lisa Drake was appointed president of Ford Energy in January this year, after most recently working as VP of technology platform programmes and EV systems.
She said the new unit will provide US-assembled BESS systems for utilities, data centers and large industrial and commercial customers in the US, having previously announced plans to invest roughly $2 billion to stand up the business.
Operations will span full battery cell manufacturing, including production of electrode coils and the assembly of modules and containers.
“For the better part of a year, we have operated quietly to build a foundation for this business,” Drake said.
“We haven’t just been planning; we have been executing — securing supply chains, readying our manufacturing sites and aligning our technology with the massive demand for domestic energy storage.”
Ford Energy’s flagship product, the Ford Energy DC block, is a standardised 20ft containerised BESS designed around 512Ah LFP prismatic cells. The company will offer two configurations — the FE-250 (two-hour system) and the FE-450 (a four-hour system).
Both integrate advanced LFP prismatic battery technology, liquid-cooled thermal management and battery management systems.
Drake said the DC block had been designed for the metrics that matter most to customers — predictable lifetime performance, ease of service and thermal stability — designed for 20-year performance.
Ford’s existing US battery manufacturing capacity in Glendale, Kentucky, will be repurposed for the new business.
Drake said the manufacturing and supply chain strategy supports a changing regulatory environment for battery energy storage and aligns with US investment tax credit requirements. It also meets material assistance and domestic content standards relevant to grid-scale storage.
“US demand for dispatchable, bankable energy storage is accelerating. The convergence of data center growth, renewable energy integration, and grid resilience requirements has created a gap in the market… that is the gap Ford Energy is built to fill.”

Battery Breaking-News Headlines
US Energy Storage Surges in Q1; Ann Arbor offers EV rebates; South Dakota launches energy storage; California tightens battery safety; Tampa Bay tackles lithium-ion waste fires; and more.
Michael C. Anderson
,
Maria Guerra
May 22, 2026
4 Min Read
Credit: Rifqyhsn, Hordieiev Roman / iStock / Getty Images Plus via Getty Images
The battery industry moves at a fast pace: The articles
Battery Technology
publishes represent only a fraction of what’s happening in this quickly evolving industry. That’s the idea behind this curated and regularly updated digest of links to breaking news related to the battery and energy storage industry. Some of these stories may become the subject of analysis by our own team—but we’re sharing them here, unfiltered, so that you can get them on your radar right away.
To keep the stream fresh, we will update this digest regularly—so please check back often!
U.S.
energy storage reached a record 9.7 gigawatt-hours
in Q1 2026, driven by data centers, AI demand, volatile electricity prices, despite federal policy hurdles.
(Reuters)
US EV tax credit updates. Courtesy of Kameleon007/iStock /Getty Images Plus.
Ann Arbor provides
limited-time rebates up to $7,500
for electric vehicle purchases, promoting affordability, reducing gas costs, and supporting climate action through sustainable transportation initiatives.
(MLive)
South Dakota's Big Stone City launches a
5 GWh thermal energy storage
facility, storing excess wind energy in carbon blocks, boosting economy and sustainability.
(APNews)
Moss Landing Power Plant in happier days in 2007. Courtesy of David Monniaux via CC BY-SA 3.0: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/.
A toxic fire at Moss Landing's
battery facility prompted stricter safety rules
, ensuring safer operations as California advances its 100% clean energy goal by 2045.
(Los Angeles Times)
Rechargeable batteries have caused an increase in
garbage truck fires in Tampa Bay
, damaging fleets and prompting safety concerns about the improper disposal of lithium-ion batteries in waste.
(Tampa Bay Times)
Earlier:
Europe’s
EV sales surged
due to soaring fuel prices from the Iran war, boosting Chinese brands and prompting automakers to consider ramping up production.
(Reuters)
Chinese EV brands are boosted by the trend. Courtesy of Hung Chin Liu/ iStock /Getty Images Plus.
Ford Energy and EDF
signed a five-year agreement for up to 20 GWh of battery storage, supporting renewable integration and enhancing North America’s grid resilience.
(Ford)
Prime Minister Carney inaugurated
Québec’s Matawinie Mine
, the G7’s largest graphite mine, strengthening Canada’s economy and battery supply chain for EVs and advanced technologies.
(Yahoo!Finance)
Recycled graphite attached to air bubbles and rising to the surface of the flotation cell at a graphite recycling system. Courtesy of Jens Schlueter/ Contributor/AFP.
GCL Technology
pivots to battery materials
, producing lithium-iron phosphate to address solar sector overcapacity, targeting renewable energy storage and expanding its battery supply chain role.
(Bloomberg)
India and the EU launched a €15.2M initiative to develop advanced
EV battery recycling
technologies, fostering resource security, circular economy, and bilateral green innovation collaboration.
(Evertiq)
Earlier:
BYD faces
battery supply constraints
as demand for flash-charge EVs accelerates, driven by new models with advanced Blade Battery technology and ultra-fast charging systems.
(CarNewsChina)
The BYD Seal uses a dual-motor all-wheel-drive system to deliver 390 kW of power. Courtesy of BYD.
GM and Samsung SDI
pause construction of the Indiana battery plant
, recalibrating EV production strategy amid cooling US demand and shifting federal incentives under CEO Mary Barra's leadership.
(GMAuthority)
Lotus
struggles to secure a European battery partner
, facing EU tariffs, complex regulations, and high costs, while seeking UK government support for localization efforts. (Bloomberg)
The Hubble Space Telescope is a project of international cooperation between NASA and the European Space Agency. Courtesy of NASA.
KULR Technology will supply
NASA-grade K1S battery systems to Argo Space
for an orbital mission, highlighting its growing role in the expanding commercial space industry.
(Kurl)
The world's
first modular battery-swapping ship
enters service, enabling faster operations with swappable battery modules, reducing dock time, and advancing electric maritime transportation efficiency.
(Yahoo!Tech)
Earlier:
In a rush? Watch the 60-second news summary of the previous week in the YouTube video below. Scroll down for the full coverage.
Ford launches "Ford Energy" to manufacture
battery energy storage systems
for data centers, utilities, and industrial customers, targeting 20 GWh annually.
(FordEnergy)
Honda reports a
$2.7B annual loss
, its first since 1957, due to restructuring costs and scaling back electric vehicle ambitions.
(NYTimes)
iPhone 16 and iPhone 16 Plus. Courtesy of Apple.
Smartphone
batteries struggle to keep up
with advanced features, frustrating users. Technological improvements and habit changes may help bridge the gap in battery performance. (CNET)
Germany's Fraunhofer IPA research team leads the RoB@t2Cell project, developing robotic systems to safely dismantle, analyze, and
repurpose EV battery cells
for reuse or recycling. (YahooTech)
Fraunhofer IPA research team leads the RoB@t2Cell project. Courtesy of Adobe Stock.
The EPA's
report to Congress highlights battery recycling best practices
, emphasizing improved collection, fire reduction, critical mineral recovery, job creation, and voluntary labeling guidelines.
(WasteDive)

title: Upstream Monitoring Redefines EV Battery Quality Control
content:
EV Batteries
Testing & Safety
Battery Manufacturing
Design & Manufacturing
Industry Trends
Upstream Monitoring Redefines EV Battery Quality Control
Early monitoring in EV battery production ensures consistent quality, scalability, and reliability by addressing issues before full-scale manufacturing.
Joachim Englund
,
Founder and Chief Technology Officer
,
ODOS
May 22, 2026
5 Min Read
Close-up of an EV cell production line.
Panasonic.
As electric vehicle (EV) production accelerates,
battery manufacturers
are facing a shift that goes beyond chemistry or energy density. The real challenge is no longer just building better batteries - it’s building them consistently, at scale, and without failure.
That shift is exposing a weakness in traditional battery manufacturing approaches. End-of-line testing, long considered the final safeguard of quality, is increasingly being asked to do too much, catching issues that arguably should never have made it that far. A growing number of manufacturers are now questioning whether this model is sustainable. The answer, in many cases, is no.
Why end-of-line testing falls short in battery manufacturing
End-of-line testing still plays an essential role, but it is inherently reactive. By the time a fault is detected, whether it’s a voltage imbalance, thermal inconsistency, or internal resistance anomaly, the root cause often lies several steps upstream.
Related:
Subaru’s 2026 Solterra EV Touring XT Delivers Power, Range, & Mixed Ride Quality
In battery assembly, small variations matter. A marginal weld, slight inconsistency in compression, or uneven thermal interface may not immediately trigger failure, but can significantly affect long-term performance. When these issues are only identified after full assembly, the consequences are costly: rework, scrap, or, in the worst case, latent defects entering the field. For an industry operating at increasingly tight margins and high volumes, that is a risk few can afford.
Shifting focus: Bringing testing upstream
What’s changing is not the need for testing, but
where and when
it happens. Live monitoring during proof-of-concept (PoC) and pre-production validation is gaining traction as manufacturers look to build a clearer picture of battery behavior before committing to full-scale production. Instead of waiting for completed battery packs to pass or fail, engineers are capturing real-time data as cells and modules are exercised under controlled conditions.
This shift fundamentally changes the role of testing, from a gatekeeper at the end of the process to a source of insight throughout it. By measuring voltage, current, temperature and internal resistance during early-stage validation, engineers can detect subtle deviations that would otherwise go unnoticed until much later. More importantly, they can begin to understand
why
those deviations occur.
Linking battery behavior to design and assembly conditions provides the traceability needed to optimize performance and reduce risk at scale. Courtesy of ODOS.
Turning data into actionable insights for battery manufacturing
Collecting data is not new. What is changing is the ability to use that data to directly inform manufacturing decisions.
Related:
EV Supply Chain Needs Strong Government Support
When live monitoring is introduced early, it becomes possible to correlate electrical and thermal behavior with specific assembly parameters, such as weld energy, pressure, or cooling conditions. Over time, this builds a clearer picture of which variables have the greatest influence on performance and reliability. This is where the real value lies.
Rather than relying on trial and error during production ramp-up, manufacturers can enter that phase with greater confidence. Processes are not just defined; they are understood and validated. That distinction is critical in an industry where scaling too quickly without sufficient process control can lead to significant quality issues.
Ensuring traceability: The backbone of reliable battery manufacturing
Of course, insight is only as good as the data behind it. Validation environments can be complex, often operating separately from production systems and sometimes under less stable conditions. Maintaining continuous, high-integrity data capture is therefore essential.
Modern monitoring architectures are starting to address this by moving part of the intelligence closer to the test itself. Data acquisition units can process signals locally, identify trends or anomalies, and buffer data during connectivity interruptions so that a complete test record is preserved.
Related:
Exploring BMW Group's Advances in AI-Driven Battery Production
Each dataset can then be tagged with identifiers such as cell ID, module reference, test condition, operator, and timestamp. This allows engineers to link electrical or thermal behavior back to the build conditions, process settings, or environment that may have influenced it. That traceability is what turns early validation data into practical evidence for process qualification, tooling adjustments, and material selection before full production begins.
This combination of edge processing, selective data handling, and structured traceability is increasingly important, not just for internal validation, but for demonstrating process robustness to partners, customers, and regulators.
EV battery assembling. Courtesy of PhonlamaiPhoto/iStock / Getty Images Plus.
Real-world impact: Early monitoring in action
The impact of this approach is already being seen in pre-production environments. In one recent validation program, a battery technology company integrated live monitoring into its module testing workflow to better understand performance before scaling to manufacturing. Engineers were able to observe real-time behavior across key parameters and identify irregularities, such as temperature gradients and voltage variation, as they occurred.
Instead of waiting for post-test analysis, issues could be investigated immediately. Test conditions were adjusted in real time, and correlations between assembly variables and performance became clearer with each cycle.
The result was not just faster iteration, but more informed iteration. By the time processes were transferred to manufacturing, they had been tested, challenged and refined under controlled conditions.
Closing the validation-to-production gap
Perhaps the most important outcome of upstream monitoring is its ability to bridge the long-standing gap between validation and production.
Too often, processes that appear stable in development behave differently under the pressures of full-scale manufacturing. By extending visibility into earlier stages, manufacturers can better anticipate how those processes will perform at volume.
For example, an imbalance detected during early module cycling may point to connection variability that would otherwise emerge as a yield issue later on. Similarly, thermal inconsistencies identified during validation can highlight design or material limitations before they impact production throughput.
Addressing these issues early is not just a matter of efficiency; it is a matter of risk management.
A shift in mindset
What this ultimately represents is a shift in mindset. Quality is no longer something that can be inspected in at the end of the line. It must be designed, validated and continuously verified throughout the process.
Live monitoring is not a silver bullet, and it does not replace established testing methods. But it does provide something that traditional approaches struggle to deliver - early, actionable insight into how batteries are actually behaving.
As production volumes increase and expectations around safety and reliability continue to rise, that insight is becoming indispensable. Manufacturers that embrace this shift are likely to find themselves better positioned, not just to scale, but to do so with confidence.
About the Author
Joachim Englund
Founder and Chief Technology Officer , ODOS
Joachim Englund is Founder and Chief Technology Officer at ODOS, with over 20 years’ experience in vehicle data acquisition and measurement systems,
specializing in instrumentation, data analysis,
and remote monitoring technologies for advanced automotive and battery development.

Joachim Englund

title: Subaru Solterra Touring XT EV Review
content:
EV Batteries
Battery Applications
Automotive & Mobility
News
Subaru’s 2026 Solterra EV Touring XT Delivers Power, Range, & Mixed Ride Quality
Subaru’s 2026 Solterra EV Touring XT offers enhanced power, improved range, & advanced features, but struggles with ride comfort and driver alerts.
Dan Carney
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 22, 2026
3 Min Read
The Subaru Solterra EV retains Subaru's expected off-road capabilities.
Subaru
At a Glance
Subaru’s Solterra XT boosts power to 338 hp
Improved battery system charges 10% to 80% in 30 mins
DriverFocus system issues excessive false alerts
A week behind the wheel of
Subaru
’s 2026 Solterra EV in the new-for-this-year Touring XT trim level gave us a good taste of what to expect for prospective owners.
This Solterra marks a significant upgrade over the first-generation model, with improvements that include an upgraded drive controller that optimizes the all-wheel-drive system’s power distribution in wet and dry conditions. Soterra’s X-Mode Dual-Mode System includes Snow/Dirt and Deep Snow/Mud modes, along with Grip Control and Downhill Assist Control.
The car’s suspension and electric power steering are retuned for improved ride and handling compared to the previous model. We’d judge the success of those efforts as being mixed, because the car still suffers a mild case of “EV ride.” That entails a harsh ride over bumps because of the stiff springs and tires needed to support the heavy battery pack.
The XT rolls on 20-inch wheels, so a lower trim level that uses the base 18-inch wheels will provide a smoother ride if that’s a concern, with the bonus of a longer driving range.
Related:
Upstream Monitoring Redefines EV Battery Quality Control
The EPA estimates that the Solterra EV Touring XT will go 278 miles when its 74.7-kilowatt-hour battery pack is fully charged. A drive into the countryside on mostly rural highways at moderate speed delivered efficiency of 3.6 miles per kWh and a range of 264 miles.
One benefit of the XT trim level is a boost from the Solterra’s
standard 233 horsepower to 338 hp
, which provides 0-60-mph acceleration of less than 5.0 seconds. Quickness isn’t necessarily a core Subaru attribute, but it is welcome when merging onto the highway.
Out on the road, the DC fast charging speed lags industry leaders, at only 150 kW, though with good efficiency, that charging rate adds range reasonably quickly. Subaru says that the Solterra’s improved battery conditioning system permits charging from 10% state of charge to 80% in half an hour.
The on-board charger for Level 2 AC charging is powerful, capable of 11 kW of juice. This exceeds the capability of the electric circuits for most home installations, however, so you probably won’t be able to maximize this without spending some money with the electrician to upgrade the circuit for your home charging station.
Driving the Solterra EV is a pretty conventional EV driving experience, with steering wheel paddles that let the driver select the regeneration level and easily adjust it on the fly. It turns out that the Solterra helpfully also adjusts the regenerative braking using the EyeSight driver assistance system.
Related:
EV Supply Chain Needs Strong Government Support
When the car notices that you have lifted off the accelerator in response to something ahead, such as a toll plaza, traffic signal, or slowing traffic, you’ll find the car decelerates to match the situation. It doesn’t bring the car to a complete stop, but it does brake almost completely automatically. This is smooth and unobtrusive because it does exactly what you were in the process of doing when you lifted off the accelerator.
The car’s driver-facing camera doesn’t work as unobtrusively. It is commendable that Subaru wants to discourage drivers from looking at their phones or playing with the infotainment screen for too long while driving. But
Subaru’s DriverFocus system
just seems to issue false warnings one after the other.
Let your eyes linger on a roadside sign, and you get a warning. Look around the upcoming bend in the road instead of directly ahead, and you get an alert. The alarms are frequent and excessive, even though I leave my phone in my pocket while driving and avoid interacting with the infotainment screen unless Sirius XMU plays that same overplayed song again.
The tested 2026 Solterra EV Touring XT model’s base price, including destination charges, is $47,005. Whether that price looks like a good value in a market where many EVs are discounted is in the eye of the beholder.
Related:
Exploring BMW Group's Advances in AI-Driven Battery Production
About the Author
Dan Carney
Senior Editor, Design News
Dan’s coverage of the auto industry over three decades has taken him to the
racetracks
, automotive engineering centers,
vehicle simulators
, wind tunnels, and crash-test labs of the world.
A member of the
North American Car, Truck, and Utility of the Year jury
, Dan also
contributes car reviews to
Popular Science
magazine, serves on the
International Engine of the Year
jury, and has judged the collegiate
Formula SAE
competition.
Dan is a winner of the
International Motor Press Association's Ken Purdy Award
for automotive writing, as well as the
National Motorsports Press Association

Washington Automotive Press Association's Golden Quill award
.
Dan, testing the 2025 Aston Martin Vanquish. ANDY MORGAN/ASTON MARTIN
He has held a
Sports Car Club of America
racing license since 1991, is an SCCA National race winner, two-time SCCA Runoffs competitor in
Formula F
, and an
Old Dominion Region
Driver of the Year award winner. Co-drove a Ford Focus 1.0-liter EcoBoost to
16 Federation Internationale de l’Automobile-accredited world speed records
over distances from just under 1km to over 4,104km at the CERAM test circuit in Mortefontaine, France.
He was also a longtime contributor to the Society of Automotive Engineers'
Automotive Engineering International
magazine.
He specializes in analyzing technical developments, particularly in the areas of motorsports, efficiency, and safety.
He has been published in
The New York Times

sports cars and contributed automotive content to the consumer finance book,

title: Lexus Engineers Balance Hybrid and EV Design
content:
Automotive & Mobility
EV Batteries
News
Lexus Engineers Tackle Challenges of Hybrid and EV Design for ES Sedan
Engineers reveal how they balanced hybrid and EV powertrains in the Lexus ES, ensuring performance and comfort while embracing sustainability.
Dan Carney
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 22, 2026
6 Min Read
The 2027 Lexus ES500h hybrid-electric vehicle.
Dan Carney
At a Glance
Engineers balanced hybrid and EV powertrains
Lexus ES combines hybrid and EV innovation
Sustainability drives Lexus ES material choices
Lexus
has made the bold decision to offer its popular ES sedan as a
hybrid-electric
or
battery-electric vehicle
only. Combining these technologies into the same platform presented engineering challenges, so
Design News
chatted with Lexus ES Chief Engineer Kohei Chiashi, to hear through his translator about developing both versions.
How long have you been working on this project?
Kohei Chiashi:
Probably around four years since the project actually started.
What did you think of the assignment to do both a hybrid and an EV version of the same vehicle?
Kohei Chiashi:
Right now, the world is changing, and all the values from customers, diversification is playing. Of course, with America in the central focus, the ES globally has a very long history, in many regions and many markets.
We wanted to make sure that even beyond, let's say, 2030 we can protect the values that the ES is provided, even beyond that. Having the hybrid and the BEV powertrains were already decided from the initial stages of development. That was a determination made to protect the values of what the ES stood for.
What was the hardest challenge you found in combining those two powertrains?
Kohei Chiashi:
One thing that was the most challenging was the renewal of the platform itself. The resulting power performance differences of putting different powertrains in the same platform were much more pronounced than originally expected. In order to hit the target performance values for each version, all the countermeasures were completely different things. They couldn't compromise performance on either variant.
It was much more challenging than I had personally expected. So an example would be collision and also dynamic performance and handling. The weight is different, the center of gravity is different, so that changed the fundamental movements of each car. That was a huge challenge for us to undertake.
Actually, as engineers, we were able to experience a lot of new discoveries, and it did strengthen the team.
Can you I think of an example of something that you had to do to balance that performance between the versions?
Kohei Chiashi:
First, the fundamental goal was to flavor all three versions of the car so that no matter which version you drove in, first and foremost, you knew right away it was an ES. You could tell it was an ES.
For example, you have the BEV, which has the main battery in the center of the platform, whereas in the hybrid, it's not located there. It's a different battery in a different location. What happens is: the way in which the vibration comes through the chassis is completely different, with different characteristics.
So we had to add extra reinforcement to the core of the frame, whereas in the BEV, you had the battery there, so it wasn't necessary. That's how we would be able to bring the two [cars’] characters closer together, as far as driving.
In the nose of the car, the engine bay, for instance, what's inside, what we've put in there, the shape, weight, and everything is different from each other. In order for us to get the acceptable collision results, test results, we had to reinforce the battery EV version instead. We required the most effective countermeasures for each version, and that became part of their expertise and their experience factor.
Is the EV's battery pack under the floor?
Kohei Chiashi:
Yes.
How much higher is the H-point for the driver?
Kohei Chiashi:
Around 140 millimeters, if you don't mind it in millimeters.
In a world where people are used to SUVs and riding higher, is that an intentional change?
Kohei Chiashi:
I personally like sedans, but the market is shrinking for sedans. And it's really a shame. We want to pump energy back into the sedan market. So I researched why the sedans aren't selling. There are many reasons for that, but the biggest one is their low field of view. It's hard to get into and egress is difficult.
We also wanted to put the battery into the platform with the ideal ingress and egress height. So we researched that, and we applied that to this car, and as a result, the higher field of view that it created as a result of that higher seat position has actually allowed us to create a field of view that hasn't been possible in previous traditional sedans.
By doing that, we're almost being able to offer a new type of sedan experience. But the ES has always had and prided itself on a spacious interior cabin space. What happened was, when we secured the extra space, it raised the overall height of the vehicle. But the other part of the ES that we had to protect is the elegant, flowing silhouette of a sedan design.
And that was the biggest part of the challenge, because when we went higher, we had to elongate.
You have a fast roof line with a sloping rear deck. It looks perfect for a hatch, but there’s no hatch.
Kohei Chiashi:
That hatch design is purely for aerodynamic performance, not just for the side profile. You'll see how the designer tapered the rear end of the cabin over the top and on all sides. That has an extremely big effect on aerodynamics.
But, I didn't want to make a coupe. I wanted to make a sedan. I was very adamant about that. I wanted to make sure to emphasize the trunk lid design while working with the designer, so aerodynamic performance as well as trying to emphasize the trunk. The traditional three-box sedan is what we are used to. From a structural standpoint, it's a very practical, very efficient setup. And the trunk lid is sort of symbolic of the traditional three-box structure. That was something that I didn't want to compromise.
Traditionally, Lexus has used a lot of natural materials, like wood and leather, inside. This car has a lot of synthetic material. Why?
Kohei Chiashi:
One [reason] is for the next-generation design languages as well. This vehicle actually includes a lot of the concepts of the next-generation direction that the Lexus brand is going to be going. So it's a precursor to the direction. Of course, material selection is included in that.
One example would be the exclusion of chrome parts. Right now, probably the only thing we have left is the wheels. As a backdrop to all that is sustainability; the efforts of the brand to move towards more sustainable materials.
Which version is your favorite?
Kohei Chiashi:
This question is hard! All three of them give the same Lexus ES driving flavor, but they still retain their own personalities, individual personalities. I’m a driver, so I like the 500e, because it is really focused on the driving pleasure.
But the version that is the most ES-like and represents everything about it, is probably the 350e. That is: All four occupants in the car have the equal amount of comfort. Especially on long drives, it's fatigue-free.
Speaking of ride comfort, how did you overcome the challenge of battery mass and low-profile tires while providing a good ride?
Kohei Chiashi: W
e took overall vehicle weight into account. Then we took vertical and lateral force on the tire rubber itself, for sidewall preference, and we chose the tire spec according to what the original vehicle's initial dynamics would be.
To stop the heavy car, you need brakes as well. So that would also affect what spec tire we chose. And the theory, the basic theory is you develop and determine tire spec based on the heaviest version of the core you have.
About the Author

title: Energy Infrastructure with a Scalable Modular Nuclear Platform
content:
Stationary Batteries
Battery Manufacturing
Trends
Industry Outlook
Interviews
Advancing Energy Infrastructure with a Modular Nuclear Energy Platform
Explore insights from Terra Innovatum’s CEO on the scalable modular nuclear platform, SOLO, in this exclusive Q&A interview.
Jake Hertz
May 21, 2026
6 Min Read
SOLO construction.
Courtesy of Terra Innovatum.
As the energy sector moves away from long-term R&D narratives toward build-ready execution, the focus has turned to technologies that can bypass the regulatory and procurement bottlenecks traditionally associated with nuclear power.
Alessandro Petruzzi
, CEO of
Terra Innovatum,
argues that the path to rapid decarbonization lies not in reinventing the wheel with exotic materials, but in optimizing proven, industrially available components into a scalable architecture.
Terra Innovatum is a developer of micro-modular reactors (MMRs), most notably the SOLO platform, a 1 MWe (megawatt-electric) baseload power system designed to fit within a standard 40-foot shipping container. Compared to traditional mega-projects that require decades of civil engineering and custom supply chains, SOLO uses rule-of-precedent design philosophy. By using commercially available, nuclear-grade materials already certified by international regulators, the company has secured an end-to-end supply chain that allows the reactor to be manufactured in a factory and deployed as a standardized infrastructure product.
Battery Technology
interviewed Petruzzi to learn about Terra Innovatum’s unique approach firsthand.
Related:
NeoVolta Q&A: Transforming Energy Storage into a Grid Asset
Alessandro Petruzzi, CEO of Terra Innovatum. Courtesy of Terra Innovatum.
What is your rule-of-precedent design philosophy, and how did it help SOLO bypass the procurement and regulatory bottlenecks that typically stall nuclear projects?
Alessandro Petruzzi, CEO of Terra Innovatum:
Our “rule-of-precedent” design philosophy is centered on de-risking both licensing
and commercial deployment. We use commercially available materials, components,
and engineering solutions that are already industrially manufactured and widely
certified by international nuclear regulators. After licensing, our reactor can be built
and deployed “tomorrow”.
Rather than relying on exotic materials, fuel types such as HALEU, or specialized
software tools that require additional regulatory approval, SOLO integrates proven
technologies and established industrial infrastructure into a new micro-modular
reactor architecture. This allows us to maintain high levels of safety and performance
while significantly simplifying both supply chain and regulatory pathways.
This approach dramatically reduces procurement risk, cost uncertainty, and licensing
friction. Our supply chain already exists, is nuclear-grade qualified, and includes
manufacturers that produce components for major nuclear programs across the
United States, Europe, and other global markets. Because the technologies and
materials involved are familiar to regulators, the review process becomes more
straightforward and predictable.
Related:
Five Key Takeaways on Ford Energy's Bold BESS Market Entry
As a result, SOLO moves away from the traditional nuclear “mega-project” model and
toward a manufactured infrastructure product—one that can be produced using
existing industrial capabilities and deployed on significantly faster timelines with far
more predictable cost structures.
How is SOLO reactor’s 1 MWe  baseload capacity a “reliability layer” that complements large-scale storage deployments rather than competing with them?
Petruzzi:
Energy storage plays an essential role in balancing intermittent renewable generation, but storage alone cannot sustain long-duration supply without a stable generation source behind it. SOLO’s 1 MWe reactor provides continuous baseload power that acts as a reliability layer within a broader energy architecture.
Rather than competing with storage, it enhances its value. Batteries and other storage technologies can absorb and dispatch energy dynamically, while SOLO provides the firm, dispatchable power needed to maintain system stability over extended periods.
Together, they create resilient microgrids capable of supporting energy-intensive infrastructure such as data centers, industrial facilities, and remote operations. In addition to the above, SOLO is designed with load-following capability, allowing the reactor to efficiently adapt to changing demand profiles. This feature enables operation across expanding or variable loads, significantly reducing the need for large-scale energy storage systems even in applications characterized by frequent and substantial load fluctuations.
Related:
AI Data Centers Stretch Tech Innovation Limits
What are the main infrastructure and siting advantages of a reactor that fits inside a standard 40-foot shipping container when targeting high-demand sectors like AI data centers?
Petruzzi:
For sectors like AI data centers, where electricity demand is surging and deployment timelines are tight, SOLO offers a ready-to-deploy energy solution post-licensing. Operators can incrementally install reactor fleets as demand grows, avoiding years-long waits for custom-built power infrastructure. This phased approach aligns energy supply with infrastructure growth, ideal for large-scale digital projects developed over time.
SOLO’s modular fleet design enhances redundancy and capital efficiency. Instead of building a full backup plant, redundancy is achieved by adding a few extra units, significantly reducing costs. Additionally, SOLO operates behind the meter, eliminating grid interconnection delays, transmission congestion, and associated charges, providing facilities with a dedicated, dispatchable power source.
With stable nuclear fuel costs and long operational periods without refueling, SOLO ensures predictable energy pricing for 15 to 45 years. This price stability offers a strategic advantage in an era of volatile energy markets, making it a transformative solution for energy-intensive industries.
Given the structural shift toward “firm power,” how does Terra Innovatum plan to use partnerships like Ameresco to grow from the current 1 MWe pilot units to the multi-unit 1 GW scalable portfolios you’ve projected?
Petruzzi:
The transition to firm, dispatchable power demands collaboration across engineering, infrastructure, and project finance. Terra Innovatum partners with experts like Ameresco, leveraging their energy infrastructure and asset management experience to advance beyond reactor design into full energy platform deployment for AI data centers, industrial clusters, and critical facilities.
We are onboarding partners to expand capabilities across federal, public-private, and commercial sectors. SOLO’s safety profile, classified by the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission as a “low-consequence reactor,” and its use of non-proliferation-compliant fuel enable deployment in diverse environments without traditional nuclear constraints.
Terra Innovatum is strengthening its U.S. presence, advancing opportunities, and combining its reactor platform with partners to develop modular fleets. These fleets aggregate reactors into distributed power clusters, achieving gigawatt-scale capacity across multiple sites. This scalable model supports infrastructure growth, meeting the evolving energy needs of digital, industrial, and strategic sectors requiring long-term, reliable power.
The SOLO reactor is designed to operate for 15 years without refueling using standard LEU fuel. How does this no-refueling model address the energy security and operational risks for off-grid industrial clients in mining or remote manufacturing?
Petruzzi:
The SOLO reactor is designed to operate for approximately 15 years without refueling. Once installed on site, the reactor module is enclosed within its biological shielding structure. At the end of the operating cycle, the reactor core can be replaced, allowing the system to continue operating for another cycle. This process can be repeated three times, enabling a total operational lifespan of up to 45 years depending on the energy requirements of the off-taker.
For remote industrial operations, fuel logistics and supply interruptions are often the greatest operational risks. Diesel generation requires continuous fuel transport over long distances, exposing operators to volatile fuel prices, supply disruptions, and significant logistical costs. At the same time, it produces substantial carbon emissions and creates environmental challenges associated with fuel transport, storage, and combustion.
SOLO’s long-duration core eliminates that dependency and carbon emissions. Once deployed, the reactor can operate continuously for more than a decade using standard low-enriched uranium (LEU), providing reliable baseload power without the need for ongoing fuel deliveries. This dramatically improves energy security for off- grid industries such as mining, remote manufacturing, and critical mineral processing.
At the same time, it reduces operational complexity, stabilizes long-term energy costs or project operational costs, and eliminates the emissions associated with diesel-based generation.
About the Author
Jake Hertz
Jake Hertz is an Electrical Engineer, Technical Writer, and Public Relations specialist for the electronics and semiconductor industries. He holds an M.S. and B.S. in EE and has designed electronics for top brands, including MakerBot, UltiMaker, Hyundai, and AT&T. He is also an analyst for the TechInsights Microprocessor Report and an alumnus of the Goldman Sachs 10KSB program.
Jake's communication business,
NanoHertz Solutions
, works with industry leaders, including NXP Semiconductors, Renesas, Synopsys, Analog Devices, Infineon, and Texas Instruments. Writing services include whitepapers, application notes, blogs, and thought-leadership pieces. PR services include campaign development and securing high-visibility media placements for clients. title: NeoVolta Q&A: Transforming Energy Storage into a Grid Asset
content:
Stationary Batteries
Industry Outlook
Interviews
NeoVolta Q&A: Transforming Energy Storage into a Grid Asset
NeoVolta's CEO shares insights on battery storage technology, highlighting its potential to become a reliable, essential asset for the future energy grid.
Jake Hertz
May 20, 2026
5 Min Read
NeoVolta’s NV Wave modular click-in residential ESS.
Courtesy of Neovolta.
With global battery storage installations jumping by 43% in 2025 and in the US hitting record growth, the industry conversation is shifting from simple solar-pairing to treating storage as the primary grid asset. Ardes Johnson, CEO of
NeoVolta,
believes that the next phase of the energy transition belongs to "firm power", or dispatchable capacity that can move beyond backup power to participate in virtual power plants (VPPs) and demand response programs.
NeoVolta is a US-based manufacturer specializing in residential and commercial energy storage solutions designed for immediate, build-ready execution. While many competitors remain buried in long-term R&D, NeoVolta has focused on high-fidelity integration of
lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) chemistry
and intelligent load management. By avoiding the price volatility of cobalt and nickel and centering their strategy on domestic manufacturing, the company is positioning itself to align with the domestic content requirements of the
One Big Beautiful Bill Act
(OBBBA) .
Battery Technology
interviewed Johnson to learn about NeoVolta and their vision for using residential batteries as proactive, grid-stabilizing assets.
Related:
Advancing Energy Infrastructure with a Modular Nuclear Energy Platform
Ardes Johnson, CEO of NeoVolta.
With global battery storage installations jumping by 43% in 2025 and record growth in the US, how is NeoVolta positioning its systems to capture this immediate surge in demand compared to long-term R&D competitors?
Ardes Johnson, CEO of NeoVolta:
NeoVolta has focused on delivering relevant products that serve multiple markets without overcomplicating the choices for installers or end-users. That approach has given us the breathing room to watch how the market is evolving and build systems that not only meet today’s demand but help set the standard for what customers should expect in this category.
Importantly, we don’t view this as a hardware race alone. Software, data visibility, installer experience, and homeowner usability are all critical to launching a complete energy platform rather than simply shipping another piece of equipment. As storage adoption accelerates, the companies that succeed will be the ones delivering a well-rounded solution — not just a product — and that has been NeoVolta’s design philosophy from the start.
How is NeoVolta navigating the OBBBA and domestic content requirements to keep your residential and light commercial systems eligible for federal investment tax credits?
Johnson:
Our strategy around domestic manufacturing predates the OBBBA. NeoVolta has long planned to establish US-based manufacturing across the full storage spectrum — from residential systems through the often underserved commercial and
industrial “middle market,” and into utility-scale storage.
Related:
Five Key Takeaways on Ford Energy's Bold BESS Market Entry
The OBBBA effectively accelerated a roadmap that was already underway. That momentum
led to the announcement of NeoVolta Power, our 2 GWh battery energy storage
manufacturing facility in Pendergrass, Georgia, which will produce both front-of-the-meter
and behind-the-meter solutions for utility and C&I markets.
On the residential side, we’ve also evolved select systems to align with OBBBA guidance and emerging domestic content frameworks. Those compliance formulas have been designed directly into our next generation of residential products, which will launch this summer.
As the industry moves toward “firm power” and dispatchable capacity, how are your battery systems specifically engineered to participate in the new market structures for demand response and virtual power plants?
Johnson:
The industry conversation is shifting from simply pairing solar with storage to recognizing that storage itself is the primary grid asset, with solar as an optional generation source layered on top. NeoVolta designs its systems with that philosophy in mind. Our batteries are engineered to function as controllable grid resources capable of participating in demand response, time-of-use optimization, and virtual power plant aggregation as those market structures continue to expand.
Related:
AI Data Centers Stretch Tech Innovation Limits
By starting with storage as the foundation and integrating intelligent load management and software coordination, we enable systems that provide both end-user resilience and dispatchable capacity that utilities and aggregators can rely on. That dual value proposition will become increasingly important as distributed batteries begin contributing more meaningfully to grid stability.
Do you see NeoVolta’s distributed, smaller-scale distribution-sited batteries helping to shorten deployment timelines by bypassing current grid constraints?
Johnson:
Distributed storage absolutely has the potential to accelerate deployment timelines, particularly compared to large centralized infrastructure projects that may require lengthy transmission upgrades.
But creating the product alone is not enough. What truly shortens deployment timelines is building the ecosystem around it. That means working with developers, community choice aggregators, utilities, and program operators that need reliable technology partners and a consistent supply of systems to scale their programs.
When those partnerships are in place, distributed batteries can be deployed much faster and closer to load centers. That creates a model where program operators can scale their offerings while manufacturers like NeoVolta scale production — ultimately leading to faster and more predictable deployment across the grid.
Given the downward trend in global battery prices and potential short-term volatility in raw materials, what is NeoVolta’s strategy to stay economically competitive while building a resilient domestic supply chain?
Johnson:
Cost competitiveness and supply chain resilience have to be addressed simultaneously. While battery prices have declined globally, the industry also recognizes the risks of overdependence on concentrated supply chains and critical minerals.
NeoVolta’s strategy is to pair efficient product design with a growing domestic manufacturing footprint. Our use of lithium iron phosphate chemistry helps avoid exposure to nickel and cobalt volatility, while our US assembly platform improves logistics, quality control, and alignment with domestic content incentives.
At the same time, we’re not only planning for what the supply chain needs today. We’re preparing for where policy and market requirements are headed. As domestic content thresholds and FEOC requirements increase over time, we anticipate the industry moving further upstream. NeoVolta is already evaluating the long-term path toward deeper vertical integration, including the eventual potential for domestic battery cell manufacturing.
About the Author
Jake Hertz
Jake Hertz is an Electrical Engineer, Technical Writer, and Public Relations specialist for the electronics and semiconductor industries. He holds an M.S. and B.S. in EE and has designed electronics for top brands, including MakerBot, UltiMaker, Hyundai, and AT&T. He is also an analyst for the TechInsights Microprocessor Report and an alumnus of the Goldman Sachs 10KSB program.
Jake's communication business,

Writing services include whitepapers, application notes, blogs, and thought-leadership pieces. PR services include campaign development and securing high-visibility media placements for clients. title: Battery Swapping Products Drive Innovation in Construction
content:
Battery Applications
Portable
Industry Outlook
Trends
News
Battery Swapping Products Drive Innovation in Construction
Swappable battery technology enhances construction equipment reliability, reduces downtime, and supports zero-emission solutions for demanding jobsite applications.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 20, 2026
3 Min Read
Swappable battery technology products transform the construction industry.
Vanguard.
Battery-swapping technology is now widespread across industries, from electric vehicles (EVs) to construction equipment. In the automotive sector,
EV battery swapping
offers a practical solution to range anxiety by enabling drivers to replace depleted batteries with fully charged ones in minutes, rather than waiting for lengthy recharging times. This same principle is now applied to other industries, where uninterrupted operation is critical. By allowing users to quickly swap batteries, this innovation minimizes downtime, simplifies power management, and enhances productivity, making it an ideal choice for applications that demand efficiency and reliability.
Applications for construction and surface preparation
In the construction and surface preparation industries, where equipment reliability and uptime are paramount, swappable battery technology is proving to be a game-changer.
Bartell Global
, in partnership with
Vanguard
, is leveraging the innovative 48V 1.5kWh Commercial Swappable Battery Pack (Si1.5) to power three new additions to its equipment lines: the B424 Edger Trowel, BC436 Power Trowel, and BCF1570 Forward Plate Compactor. These battery-powered solutions enable power swapping across different machines. As Matt Locke, VP of Sales – The Americas at Bartell Global, states, “By adopting this unified, swappable ecosystem, we are simplifying the jobsite experience — reducing the need for multiple chargers and parts while allowing crews to easily swap power across different machine types.”
Related:
SoftBank’s Zinc-Halogen Batteries Challenge LFP in AI Data Centers
The partnership between these two companies marks a significant step forward in integrating swappable battery technology into professional-grade equipment. Therefore, ensuring that contractors can easily transport and maneuver equipment while maintaining the energy required for demanding applications. Additionally, the Si1.5 is backed by engineering expertise, product support, and an 8-year limited commercial warranty, providing long-term confidence for dealers, rental partners, and contractors alike.
Vanguard Si1.5 battery pack design
The Vanguard Si1.5 battery pack is designed to meet the high-productivity demands of modern jobsites. According to the company, Its fast-charging capability — reaching 80% charge in approximately 50 minutes — allows operators to recharge during natural breaks, such as lunch or travel time, effectively eliminating downtime. This maintenance-free battery is engineered and assembled in the USA, ensuring reliability and durability for commercial use. By integrating this technology into Bartell Global’s equipment, contractors gain access to zero-emission solutions that are ideal for noise-sensitive and enclosed environments, such as schools, hospitals, and residential areas.
Related:
From Apollo to Artemis II: Batteries in Space!
48V 1.5kWh commercial battery swappable battery pack. Courtesy of Vanguard.
Standardizing battery swappable platforms
Bartell Global’s adoption of the Vanguard Si1.5 battery pack reflects its commitment to evolving with industry needs. By centering its electric portfolio around a standardized, swappable power platform, the company is creating a scalable foundation for future innovation. This approach allows Bartell Global to expand battery integration across additional equipment lines, including compaction, concrete finishing, and surface preparation platforms. As Matt Locke, VP of Sales – The Americas at Bartell Global, notes, “By adopting this unified, swappable ecosystem, we are simplifying the jobsite experience — reducing the need for multiple chargers and parts while allowing crews to easily swap power across different machine types.”
Swappable battery technology is set to transform the construction industry by enabling greater efficiency, sustainability, and adaptability on jobsites. As lithium-ion battery prices fall, this innovation is becoming more accessible, allowing contractors to integrate electric solutions into their workflows. As demand for zero-emission equipment grows, advancements in battery capacity and portability will further reduce downtime and support the industry's shift toward greener practices, ensuring that swappable batteries become a vital component of construction's future.
Related:
Understanding High Impedance and Its Risks for Pacemakers
About the Author
Maria Guerra
Senior Editor-Battery Technology, Informa Markets
Maria L. Guerra is a Senior Editor at
Battery Technology
, blending her engineering background with a passion for technology journalism. She holds a Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering from Universidad Metropolitana in Caracas, Venezuela, and a Master of Science in Electrical Engineering from NYU Tandon School of Engineering.
Guerra's career spans power systems engineering, oil & gas consulting, and technical writing and editing. She worked for several years at Kellogg, Brown & Root Ltd. (KBR) in the UK, conducting power system studies and providing technical support for offshore and onshore projects. During her internship at Pequiven S.A. in Venezuela, she trained neural networks using MATLAB (Kohonen Map) to estimate electrical circuit variables for oil wells (e.g., voltage, current, and power).
In addition to her engineering roles, Guerra worked as a freelance technical editor, refining complex technical content for clarity and accuracy. She later became a Power/Analog Editor at

She is passionate about uncovering the stories that shape the future of electrification, from cutting-edge battery innovations to policy shifts driving sustainable energy.

title: Solid-State Batteries in Europe
content:
Materials
Industry Outlook
Trends
News
Solid-State Batteries: The Battery Show Europe Plots the Path to Scale
Explore solid-state battery readiness, challenges, and Europe’s strategy for scaling this transformative EV technology.
Dan Carney
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 19, 2026
2 Min Read
Mercedes-Benz solid-state battery logo.
Dan Carney
At a Glance
Assess solid-state battery maturity and readiness
Learn commercialization timelines and bottlenecks
Compare solid-state to lithium-ion and alternatives
One of the biggest questions facing the global auto industry is when
solid-state batteries
will arrive in scale. This will be a transformative event that makes EVs even more economically attractive and could be the necessary step to permit the sunsetting of combustion power.
Attendees to
The Battery Show Europe 2026
at Messe Stuttgart, June 9-11, can get an update on progress toward this long-sought goal in the session, “
Solid-State Batteries Beyond the Hype: Readiness, Reality and Europe’s Path to Scale
” on Wednesday, June 10
th
.  You can sign up here:
Registration
.
The three panelists in this session will discuss the fact that while solid-state batteries are frequently positioned as the next major breakthrough in EV performance, safety, and energy density, commercial deployment has remained stubbornly out of reach despite years of investment and announcements. The panel will look at where solid-state technology genuinely stands today, separating technical reality from long-term ambition.
The discussion will assess what has changed, what challenges still block scale-up, and how solid-state fits into Europe’s broader mobility, industrial, and policy objectives. They will also question whether solid-state is the right near-term bet, or whether alternative chemistries may deliver impact sooner.
They promise three outcomes to benefit attendees:
A realistic assessment of solid-state technical maturity, manufacturing readiness, and remaining bottlenecks
Clear-eyed timelines for commercialization and what would need to change to accelerate them
How solid-state compares to advanced lithium-ion and emerging alternatives in terms of cost, scale, and strategic fit for Europe
The speakers are:
Dr. Alex Yu
, CTO & Co-Founder at Factorial Inc.
Mr. Jean-Baptiste Behaghel
, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer at Blue Solutions
Mr. Vincent Yang
, CEO at Prologium
Their discussion will be moderated by
Dr. Xiaohan Wu
, Lead Expert - Battery Technology at Porsche Consulting
Event Details:
Date: Wednesday, 10 June
Time: 9:30 AM - 10:30 AM CET
Location:
Room C2 - Keynotes
The Battery Show Europe | Electric & Hybrid Vehicle Technology Expo Europe
Jun 9, 2026 TO Jun 11, 2026
|
Messe Stuttgart | Stuttgart, Germany
Join the leading meeting place for the advanced battery and H/EV technology community. Meet manufacturers, suppliers, engineers, thought leaders and decision-makers for a conference and trade fair focused on the latest developments in the advanced battery and automotive industries.

title: EV Supply Chain Needs Strong EV Government Support
content:
EV Batteries
Battery Manufacturing
Industry Outlook
Trends
EV Supply Chain Needs Strong Government Support
Lotus Technology Inc.’s struggles in battery manufacturing highlight the urgent need for EV government support to boost the EV supply chain.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 19, 2026
3 Min Read
Emeya is Lotus’ first electric hyper-GT.
Courtesy of Lotus.
The push to establish a robust electric vehicle (EV) supply chain has highlighted the indispensable role of government support in fostering innovation, securing investments, and ensuring long-term sustainability. The challenges faced by Lotus Technology Inc., as reported by
Bloomberg
, underscore the urgent need for strategic government intervention to secure the future of the EV sector.
Lotus Technology’s EV battery struggles in Europe
Lotus
, owned by China’s Zhejiang Geely Holding Group, is struggling to find a European partner to build EV batteries. This partnership would help the company localize production and comply with the European Union’s complex content rules. “In China, you may instantly find five to six right partners,” stated Lotus CEO Qingfeng Feng in an interview with Bloomberg. “But in the EU it simply didn’t work as we expect.” This bold contrast highlights the lack of established battery manufacturing infrastructure in the UK and Europe, leaving companies like Lotus at a disadvantage.
Related:
Upstream Monitoring Redefines EV Battery Quality Control
Europe’s efforts to build a competitive EV supply chain are under pressure. The collapse of battery startups such as Britishvolt and
Northvolt
has further exposed the fragility of the region’s EV battery ambitions.
Learning from the Britishvolt bankruptcy
Britishvolt, once a symbol of the UK’s EV aspirations,
filed for administration in 2023
after failing to secure sufficient private investment. The company had planned to build a $3.9B gigafactory in Northumberland, which would have produced 300,000 battery cells annually and created thousands of jobs. However, despite an initial government grant and agreements with automakers like Lotus, Britishvolt’s lack of a clear sales strategy and financial backing led to its downfall.
Lotus had previously agreed to a deal with Britishvolt, but the startup’s collapse has left the luxury automaker scrambling for alternatives. This situation underscores the need for reliable funding and stronger government support to reduce risks in high-stakes EV ventures.
The role of government support
Lotus is not solely focusing on EVs; it is also investing in hybrid technology as part of its strategy to navigate the evolving automotive landscape. The company plans to produce plug-in hybrid versions of the Emira and a new hybrid supercar, the Type 135, at its Hethel factory near Norwich. This dual focus reflects an approach to meeting diverse market demands while managing production costs and regulatory challenges.
Meanwhile, many years ago, China’s government started adopting a strategic approach to battery dominance. Galyen Energy’s Robert (Bob) Galyen, former CTO of Contemporary Amperex Technology (CATL), highlighted at the latest Battery Show North America that
China’s systematic approach to battery dominance
has government support as one of its principal pillars, including robust subsidies, guaranteed purchases of early, expensive technology, and the fostering of a domestic supply chain. This has positioned China as a global leader in battery innovation and production.
Related:
Subaru’s 2026 Solterra EV Touring XT Delivers Power, Range, & Mixed Ride Quality
Lotus is seeking investment from the UK government to support its Hethel factory near Norwich. “Our team is currently actively making feasibility studies and investigating the potential solutions,” Feng told Bloomberg. “However, we would also like to get some help and support from the UK government.” This plea for public funding highlights the critical role of government intervention in fostering a sustainable EV ecosystem.
Without the government’s sustained, strategic support, countries risk falling further behind in the race for EV dominance. Lotus’s current challenges serve as an absolute reminder of the critical role government support plays in building a competitive EV supply chain.

title: Battery Safety, Thermal Management at Battery Show Europe 2026
content:
Thermal Management
Testing & Safety
News
Thermal Management & Safety Issues Under Scope at The Battery Show Europe
Technical sessions examine safety challenges and thermal management innovations that are critical to battery cells and stationary battery systems.
Spencer Chin
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 19, 2026
4 Min Read
Battery cell and system thermal and safety issues will be the focus of several sessions at The Battery Show Europe.
Battery Show Europe
Battery fires and outright failures are an unfortunate reality that engineers are trying to lessen in future battery cells. At the upcoming
The Battery Show Europe
in Stuttgart, Germany June 9 through 11, several sessions will look at the progress being made to resolve long-standing issues such as thermal runaway and cell microcracking. Here are several sessions anyone concerned about battery safety and reliability should attend.
Designing Thermal Management and Safety into Battery Packs for Next-Generation Batteries
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=167
Thursday, 11 June
2:30 PM - 3:00 PM CET
As battery chemistries evolve, pack-level thermal management and safety strategies must adapt to new operating profiles, mechanical behaviours and failure modes. While next-generation cells promise improvements in intrinsic safety and performance, they also introduce new design challenges that can only be addressed through collaborative innovation in structural design and materials.
This session uses a next-generation battery pack development program as a case study to examine how thermal management, containment and structural design choices are changing at pack level. The focus is on the engineering rationale behind key design decisions, the trade-offs encountered during testing and validation, and the lessons learned when balancing thermal protection, weight and manufacturability.
Cathode Microcracking: Understanding and Preventing Long-Term Degradation in Commercial Cells
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=42
Tuesday, 9 June
2:00 PM - 3:00 PM CET
As the push for energy density drives increases in the nickel content of NMC and NCA cathodes, mechanical stress and microcracking has become a major challenge that can lead to non-linear degradation and rollover failure. As service lifetimes of EV batteries and BESS systems continually improve, long-term degradation studies are needed that assess the effectiveness of strategies to prevent microcracking over thousands of cycles and decades of service life.  This presentation uses advanced x-ray imaging and diffraction to characterize the surprising long-term effects of microcracking in commercially manufactured pouch cells that have been extensively cycled in Dr. Jeff Dahn’s lab at Dalhousie University. The presentation will show how operating conditions and materials selection can dramatically impact the extent of microcracking, including a remarkable single-crystal cell that has been cycled more than 20,000 times over a period of 6 years.
From Propagation to Prevention: Materials Solutions for Improved Battery Safety
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=225
Tuesday, 9 June
1:00 PM - 1:45 PM CET
This session will discuss the challenge of maintaining battery safety while reducing design constraints at the module level, and will explore materials-based solutions for mitigating risks such as thermal runaway propagation, pressure control and ingress management. Details around testing under real-world conditions will be presented and discussed.
BESS Safety: Trends in Thermal Management, Fire & Explosion Protection
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=196
Wednesday, 10 June
12:15 PM - 12:30 PM CET
Amid several high-profile stationary battery energy storage system (BESS) fire incidents, scrutiny of BESS safety has intensified in recent years. Risks to personnel, financial losses, asset damage, and environmental impact are all driving demand for safer Li-ion BESS technologies.
As the German market shifts from a predominantly behind-the-meter (BTM) focus toward larger utility-scale projects, developers and integrators must prepare for the expansion of European supply chains for thermal management, fire protection, and explosion mitigation materials and components. In parallel, the rapid growth of data centers across Germany and Europe will place greater emphasis on deploying safer BESS technologies at hyperscale facilities. By applying lessons learned from past safety incidents, stakeholders can help safeguard Germany’s market from large-scale BESS safety events that could otherwise hinder domestic growth.
Increasing Risk of Aged Cells – A Comparison of Cell Chemistries
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=109
Tuesday, 9 June
3:30 PM - 4:00 PM CET
This sessions will overview the safety aspects of lithium-ion cells with the typical sequence of a thermal runaway. Presentation of research results how the risk of a thermal runaway is increasing over lifetime due to degradation for different cell chemistries like NMC, LFP and LTO. Safety standards and test often do not always consider this aspect of growing risk.
How To Design Simulate and Homologate IEC62619 With NMC Cells: Thermal Runaway in Railway Applications
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=112
Thursday, 11 June
2:00 PM - 2:30 PM CET
As regulatory scrutiny around battery safety intensifies, pack developers must demonstrate not only performance but controlled failure behaviour. Thermal runaway containment is no longer a laboratory concern but a certification requirement, particularly in transport sectors with strict homologation standards such as rail.
This session will examine how early-stage 3D multiphysics simulation, combined with incremental physical testing, can de-risk battery pack design by predicting and validating thermal propagation behavior in high-energy NMC systems. Using 100 kWh HEV pack architecture as a case study, the presentation will outline how numerical modelling and experimental correlation inform safer material and structural decisions.
The Battery Show Europe | Electric & Hybrid Vehicle Technology Expo Europe
Jun 9, 2026 TO Jun 11, 2026
|
Messe Stuttgart | Stuttgart, Germany
Join the leading meeting place for the advanced battery and H/EV technology community. Meet manufacturers, suppliers, engineers, thought leaders and decision-makers for a conference and trade fair focused on the latest developments in the advanced battery and automotive industries.

About the Author
Spencer Chin
Senior Editor, Design News
Spencer Chin is a Senior Editor for Design News, covering the electronics beat, which includes semiconductors, components, power, embedded systems, artificial intelligence, augmented and virtual reality, and other related subjects.
When Spencer began covering electronics years ago, there was no internet, no cell phones, let alone smartphones, and PCs were emerging but not widespread. Robots and artificial intelligence? Only in a science fiction film. Modems were a curiosity requiring patience fumbling with connection scripts, and Bluetooth and wireless communications did not exist. Spencer has witnessed and covered, first-hand, many developments in the underpinnings of these modern technologies that we take for granted.
Now, we're in the age of artificial intelligence, autonomous vehicles, high-speed signaling hardware, FPGAs, microcontrollers, and advanced design tools. Spencer is again in the trenches covering these developments, and looking forward to the technologies of the future.
At the same time, Spencer has adapted his reporting and editing style to the digital age. It's not just news stories and features but also social media posts, and videos, as seen below. Adapting to 21st-century media consumption habits has kept Spencer on his toes learning new tools and skills, though he admits it is sometimes hard to teach an old dog new tricks.
Spencer's long list of editorial stops reads like a Who's Who in technical content brands, including
Electronic Products
, Electronic Buyers News,
EE Times
,
Power Electronics
, and
NASA Tech Briefs
. He is proud to be the go-to electronics editor at Design News.
