title: ProLogium CEO Shares Insights on Solid-State Battery Leadership
content:
Lithium-Ion Batteries
Battery Manufacturing
Interviews
ProLogium CEO Shares Insights on Solid-State Battery Leadership
Vincent Yang, CEO of ProLogium, discusses solid-state battery advancements, manufacturing strategies, and the future of energy storage.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 18, 2026
6 Min Read
ProLogium’s solid-state battery cell.
Courtesy of ProLogium.
In this interview with Battery Technology, Vincent Yang, CEO of
ProLogium
, discusses advancements in solid-state battery technology, key manufacturing strategies, and the company's vision for the future of energy storage.
ProLogium has achieved volume production of solid-state batteries while many competitors remain in the lab. What were the key technological breakthroughs or strategic decisions that enabled ProLogium to reach this milestone ahead of others?
Vincent Yang, Founder & CEO of ProLogium:
We attribute our progress to our long-term strategy focused on innovation and manufacturing capabilities since ProLogium’s inception two decades ago.
Our uniqueness is built on the full integration of two critical components. We developed a superfluidized all-inorganic electrolyte, made without rare elements, that achieves world-record conductivity and features an Active Safety Mechanism (ASM) that automatically decomposes to eliminate fire risk at the material level.
Related:
Toshiba’s SCiB Battery Comparison with Conventional Li-ion Batteries
We paired this with a highly stable ceramic separator that prevents the charged cathode and anode from making contact, thereby stopping dangerous thermal chain reactions. Underpinned by this triple safety, high energy density can be continuously pursued from the chemical level and system, with reactive cathode and anode active materials like NMC 955, silicon-based anode, and Li-metal.
Our approach is also more cost competitive. With silicon anode unit energy cost at two-thirds that of graphite, and proprietary electrolyte synthesis cost approaching parity with liquid electrolytes, and dry room requirements of 30%-40% of a conventional EV battery, production is more efficient and cost effective. Strategically, our key tactics have been to prove our manufacturing readiness in parallel with our R&D. We have re-engineered how solid-state batteries are manufactured — eliminating the most complex and time-consuming steps in conventional battery production to achieve structural advantages in cost, speed, and throughput.
For example, we reduced the core production process from 17 to 11 steps by eliminating separator film placement, liquid electrolyte injection, vacuum soaking, and degassing. Our proprietary hot flow dehydration takes 8 minutes versus 8 hours compared to traditional processes. While others remained in the lab, still struggling to overcome high manufacturing costs and the need for external pressure during operation, ProLogium has already addressed these challenges with shipment of over 2.4 million cells to date. This includes approximately 800,000 cells delivered from our new GWh-class production line in Taiwan since it came online in Q3 2024.
Related:
Latest LFP Battery Projects Driving Safety, Sustainability, and Savings
With the new gigafactory in Dunkirk, France, how does ProLogium plan to strengthen the global battery supply chain? What role will this facility play in meeting the growing demand for EV batteries and other applications?
Yang:
The Dunkirk gigafactory is a cornerstone of our global expansion strategy and a direct response to Europe's push for a resilient, sovereign battery supply chain. This facility is purpose-built for mass-production of our fourth-generation superfluidized inorganic battery for the European and global market, applying the proven manufacturing framework from our operational gigafactory in Taiwan.
The first fab will feature a production capacity of 4 GWh a year, rising gradually to 12 GWh upon the completion of Phase 2. The Dunkirk site will establish a local manufacturing system—covering production, quality, and supply-chain collaboration—designed around an all-inorganic solid-state electrolyte architecture to balance safety, performance, and manufacturability at scale. The new plant will be integrated into the broader "Battery Valley" in Northern France, allowing us to leverage a rich ecosystem of talent, materials, recycling, and technology partners.
Related:
Hawaii's 'Maui Method' Transforms Lithium-Ion Battery Disposal, Earns EPA Endorsement
By localizing production with the strong backing of the French government—including a historic €1.5 billion subsidy—and utilizing the region's low-carbon, clean power, we are building a European manufacturing backbone to reliably meet the growing demand for not only EV batteries but a wide range of other applications that require safer, higher-performance energy.
Rendering of the Dunkirk gigafactory. Courtesy of ProLogium.
ProLogium is adapting its solid-state technology for applications beyond EVs, such as data centers, aerospace, and energy storage systems. What are the unique challenges and opportunities in these sectors, and how does ProLogium plan to address them?
Yang:
Emerging industries like aerospace, AI infrastructure, robotics, mobile energy, and defense are driving new battery demands beyond EVs. Next-gen batteries must offer high energy density, safety, extreme environment performance, balanced efficiency, and cost-effectiveness to meet these needs.
Intrinsic safety is a key feature of our technology that is required by many of these emerging applications given the high value-add nature of these industries. Meanwhile, each industry has unique system-level requirements. For example, battery weight is important for aerospace applications such as aircraft, eVTOL, satellite and drones where customers are looking for batteries that are 30-50% lighter for the same capacity.
In ESS (energy storage systems), 2x to 5x power capability and low heat generation are critical in ensuring optimal performance and cost effectiveness at data centers. For robotics, longer mission runtime and faster charging speeds are highly desired. Our strategy addresses this through a partner-led model. We focus on manufacturing the most technically demanding part—the core internal cell—which our domain-specialist partners then handle for final system integration and deployment.
For example, our partnership with Delta Electronics is focused on developing battery management systems for energy storage and AI data centers, while our collaboration with Kyushu Electric Power is creating specialized modules for zero-emission construction equipment. This partner-driven ecosystem strategy is similar to how we work with Tier 1 partners such as Rimac and FEV in the EV market — leveraging their platforms and integration capabilities to showcase the strengths of our battery cells, bring them into end-customer EV platforms, and accelerate market penetration.
While tariffs have significantly influenced the production and supply chain of LFP batteries, how are they affecting the development and adoption of next-generation technologies like solid-state batteries? Are there specific policy changes that could accelerate innovation and global deployment?
Yang:
Tariffs have not meaningfully impacted our production and supply chain. Overall, our strategy is designed to build a resilient global supply chain that is less vulnerable to regional dependency. No LFP cathode or graphite anode is used in our chemistry. Instead, our battery chemistry, which does not use rare materials, allows for greater supply chain resilience, especially for customers who are looking for alternative battery supply options.
There is an imbalance in terms of policy support for battery innovation across different markets. During an age where energy resilience and industrial sovereignty are top of mind, there are opportunities for public and private players to work together and push innovation forward in the battery space. The French government's "France 2030" plan, which is backing our Dunkirk project, is a great example. This kind of strong, long-term government support is crucial for building local manufacturing capabilities and ensuring that next-generation technologies can scale from the lab to the factory floor.
Given the rapid advancements and competitive developments in the battery space, where do you see the industry heading in the next 5 years? What are the biggest hurdles that need to be overcome for solid-state batteries to become the mainstream choice?
Yang:
The next five years will be defined by a race to bring next-generation batteries from testing to production. The current slowdown in EV growth isn't just about cost; fundamentally, it's a battery performance problem. For EVs to achieve mass adoption, batteries must deliver predictable range in all temperatures, eliminate safety risks, and offer gasoline-like refueling in minutes, not hours. We firmly believe that solid-state batteries are a key solution to address these issues.
However, for any new technology to become mainstream, it must overcome two hurdles: it must prove consistent performance in the lab and in real life, and it must prove its viability for mass production at a competitive cost. Many are still stuck at the first hurdle. We believe that with proven chemistry, established manufacturing capability, and a clear path to cost parity with conventional batteries, we have a strong plan to clear both.
About the Author
Maria Guerra
Senior Editor-Battery Technology, Informa Markets
Maria L. Guerra is a Senior Editor at
Battery Technology
, blending her engineering background with a passion for technology journalism. She holds a Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering from Universidad Metropolitana in Caracas, Venezuela, and a Master of Science in Electrical Engineering from NYU Tandon School of Engineering.
Guerra's career spans power systems engineering, oil & gas consulting, and technical writing and editing. She worked for several years at Kellogg, Brown & Root Ltd. (KBR) in the UK, conducting power system studies and providing technical support for offshore and onshore projects. During her internship at Pequiven S.A. in Venezuela, she trained neural networks using MATLAB (Kohonen Map) to estimate electrical circuit variables for oil wells (e.g., voltage, current, and power).
In addition to her engineering roles, Guerra worked as a freelance technical editor, refining complex technical content for clarity and accuracy. She later became a Power/Analog Editor at
Electronic Design
, covering advancements in the semiconductor market, focusing on power/analog electronics systems.
At
Battery Technology
, Guerra now delivers in-depth coverage of battery manufacturing, EV advancements, energy storage systems, and the evolving landscape of critical minerals and second-life batteries. She is passionate about uncovering the stories that shape the future of electrification, from cutting-edge battery innovations to policy shifts driving sustainable energy.
Guerra is also a member of
The Battery Show North America Advisory Board
, where she contributes her expertise to advising on industry trends and speaker selection for The Battery Show North America, while helping to shape discussions on battery technology and electrification. She was part of the award-winning Battery Technology team recognized with a
Neal Award for Best Coverage of the 2024 Election

title: Five Key Takeaways on Ford Energy's Bold BESS Market Entry
content:
Stationary Batteries
EV Batteries
Battery Applications
Battery Manufacturing
Industry Trends
Five Key Takeaways on Ford Energy's Bold BESS Market Entry
Discover five key takeaways on Ford Energy's BESS market entry, leveraging CATL technology for innovative energy storage solutions.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 15, 2026
5 Slides
Start slideshow
Ford Energy marks the automaker's official entry into the stationary energy storage market.
Scott Olson / Staff/Getty Images News
Tesla has long been the undisputed leader among automotive original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) in the battery energy storage systems (BESS) market. Its utility-scale Megapack and residential Powerwall systems have not only transformed the energy storage business but also become a significant revenue driver for Tesla's total revenue. Tesla’s dominance has set a high bar for competitors, but now, a new challenger has entered the arena: Ford Motor Company.
Ford’s new strategy: Entering the BESS market with "Ford Energy"
At the end of 2025, Ford announced its
new strategy
: to enter the battery energy storage systems (BESS) market. This bold move was made official with the formal introduction of "
Ford Energy
," a new subsidiary dedicated to energy storage solutions. This marks a significant pivot for the automaker as it seeks to diversify its business and capitalize on the rapidly growing demand for energy storage solutions. Ford’s new strategy positions the company to compete in a sector poised for high growth and profitability, challenging Tesla’s dominance.
Related:
Advancing Energy Infrastructure with a Modular Nuclear Energy Platform
Ford’s journey into the battery industry has been marked by bold moves. In 2021, the company partnered with SK Innovation in an $11.4 billion joint venture (JV) to build three massive gigafactories in the American South under the "BlueOval SK" banner. This historic investment promised 129 gigawatt-hours (GWh) of annual capacity and was the largest manufacturing investment in Ford’s 118-year history. However, on November, 2025, Ford and SK On
announced the dissolution of their joint venture
, reflecting the volatility of the current battery market. The two companies have since divided the assets and now operate independently.
Amid this backdrop, Ford has made another significant pivot with the official launch of its new subsidiary, Ford Energy, signaling its ambition to diversify and capitalize on the high-growth BESS market. Click through to see the slideshow to learn five key takeaways from this development:

title: Ohio State Wins Battery Workforce Challenge Year Three
content:
Design & Manufacturing
EV Batteries
Battery Manufacturing
Thermal Management
News
Ohio State Wins Battery Workforce Challenge Year Three
The Ohio State University and Columbus State Community College win Year Three of the Battery Workforce Challenge, showcasing innovation in EV battery design.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 14, 2026
2 Min Read
The Ohio State University & Columbus State Community College team has been named the Year Three winner of the Battery Workforce Challenge.
Courtesy of The Ohio State University.
The Ohio State University and Columbus State Community College team has
emerged
as the Year Three winner of the prestigious
Battery Workforce Challenge
, a three-year collegiate engineering competition that brought together 11 teams from across North America. The challenge, managed by Argonne National Laboratory and sponsored by the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and Stellantis, tasked participants with designing and building an advanced battery pack for the commercial Ram ProMaster EV platform.
The competition, which spanned three years, provided students with invaluable hands-on experience in battery design, validation, safety, and vehicle integration. Over the course of the program, participants tackled real-world engineering challenges, progressing through technical milestones that mirrored the demands of the rapidly evolving mobility industry. The competition emphasized not only technical expertise but also teamwork, project management, and innovative problem-solving.
Related:
Breakthrough Small-Scale Power: 7 Promising Micro-Battery Technologies
Recognizing Excellence Across North America
In the first year, teams focused on establishing their architecture strategy, laying the foundation for their designs. Year two saw participants delve into design trade-offs, while the final year required teams to address battery packaging trade-offs, finalize their designs, and develop manufacturing supply strategies. The Ohio State University and Columbus State Community College team stood out for their exceptional project-management plan, team cohesiveness, and efficiency in completing each phase of the challenge.
McMaster University and Mohawk College secured second place, while two teams tied for third: the Rose-Hulman Institute of Technology and Ivy Tech Community College team, and the University of Alabama and Shelton State Community College team.
Over the past three years, students progressed through a series of technical milestones that reflect contemporary challenges in the industry. Courtesy of The Ohio State University.
Electrical engineering graduate student Gabriel Lamba credited the Battery Workforce Challenge with setting him up for success after graduation. “This experience has been instrumental in preparing me for my career by providing valuable education, industry connections, and hands-on experience."
As one of the team’s faculty advisors, Research Assistant Professor at The Ohio State University, Matilde D’Arpino was inspired by the students’ growth, teamwork, and accomplishments. “They conceptualized, prototyped, and integrated a beautifully engineered high-voltage battery pack, working hand-in-hand with experts from Stellantis and other industry partners throughout the process,” she said. “They learned to be engineers.”
Related:
How Advanced Battery Systems Will Enable the Humanoid Robot Revolution
The Battery Workforce Challenge not only highlighted the ingenuity and dedication of the next generation of engineers but also served as a launchpad for many participants, some of whom have already joined Stellantis to begin their professional careers. This initiative underscores the importance of fostering talent to meet the growing demands of the electric vehicle industry.

title: Walmart EV Charging Network Expands Nationwide
content:
Charging
EV Batteries
Automotive & Mobility
News
Walmart Expands EV Charging Network With ABB Fast Chargers Across US Stores
Walmart launches its own EV fast-charging network with ABB chargers, promising affordable rates and integration with its app.
Dan Carney
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 13, 2026
3 Min Read
One of Walmart's new ABB A400 chargers in Apache Junction, AZ.
ABB E-Mobility
At a Glance
Walmart integrates EV charging with shopping
ABB chargers deliver up to 400 kW split between two cars
Walmart promises lower costs for EV charging
Three years after announcing that the big-box retailer would
build out its own EV fast-charging network
, Walmart has begun opening charging stations at some of its stores, using
ABB
E-Mobility’s latest A400 All-in-One fast chargers.
Walmart says that it aims to provide convenient charging for everyday use, with between eight and 16 fast-charging stalls featuring the 400-kilowatt ABB chargers. The stations are already open in some Arizona locations, including Mesa, Apache Junction, and Tempe, with more
locations planned
across the Phoenix metro area this year. Sites in Colorado, Florida, and Georgia are next in line for a planned nationwide rollout.
Electrify America
already has charging stations at many Walmart locations, so it isn’t clear what benefit Walmart’s own chargers will provide at this point. When the company made its announcement in 2023, Electrify America’s charging network was not well-regarded because of the state of repair of many of its chargers.
Since then, EA seems to have made progress on the upkeep of its chargers, so the benefit of Walmart operating its own stations isn’t obvious. A Walmart spokesperson provided a statement on this, saying, “What sets Walmart apart is the integration into the shopping experience. Customers can charge while they shop, access stations through the Walmart app they already use, and benefit from affordable pricing backed by Walmart’s Every Day Low Price approach.”
Of course, drivers can already do these things while using Electrify America chargers, although those won’t be connected to the Walmart mobile app.
The ABB A400 All-in-One fast chargers feature one Combined Charge System cable and one Tesla-style North American Charging Standard charging cable per charging station. It is capable of delivering a maximum of 400 kW, but that is split between the two cables, so if there are two cars connected, each tops out at 200 kW.
And as a reminder that EV charging remains a work in progress that leaves drivers to discover new challenges seemingly daily, owners of Teslas built before 2021 will need to get a firmware update for their cars to be able to use the chargers, according to a note from Walmart posted on the EV charging site Plugshare.com.
Walmart promises lower charging costs, which would be welcome. DC fast-charging EVs can make driving an EV as expensive as driving a gas car. A
price check on the Walmart chargers
using Plugshare shows that between 8 am and 11 pm, Walmart charges 58 cents a kilowatt, which is in line with the cost at other existing EV charging networks. Overnight, the price drops to 34 cents. For comparison, the rate at a nearby EVGo fast charger peaks at 54 cents.
EV charging analyst Paren
reports that, by the end of 2025, more than 30% of installed DC fast chargers were capable of 300kW charging levels or more, with another 50% in the 100-kW to 300-kW range. They also found that
the top locations where people eat
while charging are Starbucks, Subway, and Burger King. Walmart didn’t make the Top 10 list.
DAN CARNEY
There are more than 5,200 Walmart and Sam’s Club stores across the United States, putting 90% of Americans within 10 miles of a Walmart location, according to the company.
About the Author
Dan Carney
Senior Editor, Design News
Dan’s coverage of the auto industry over three decades has taken him to the
racetracks
, automotive engineering centers,
vehicle simulators
, wind tunnels, and crash-test labs of the world.
A member of the
North American Car, Truck, and Utility of the Year jury
, Dan also
contributes car reviews to
Popular Science
magazine, serves on the
International Engine of the Year
jury, and has judged the collegiate
Formula SAE
competition.
Dan is a winner of the
International Motor Press Association's Ken Purdy Award
for automotive writing, as well as the
National Motorsports Press Association
's award for magazine writing and the
Washington Automotive Press Association's Golden Quill award
.
Dan, testing the 2025 Aston Martin Vanquish. ANDY MORGAN/ASTON MARTIN
He has held a
Sports Car Club of America
racing license since 1991, is an SCCA National race winner, two-time SCCA Runoffs competitor in
Formula F
, and an
Old Dominion Region
Driver of the Year award winner. Co-drove a Ford Focus 1.0-liter EcoBoost to
16 Federation Internationale de l’Automobile-accredited world speed records
over distances from just under 1km to over 4,104km at the CERAM test circuit in Mortefontaine, France.
He was also a longtime contributor to the Society of Automotive Engineers'
Automotive Engineering International
magazine.
He specializes in analyzing technical developments, particularly in the areas of motorsports, efficiency, and safety.
He has been published in
The New York Times
,
NBC News
,
Motor Trend
,
Popular Mechanics
,
The Washington Post
,
Hagerty
,
AutoTrader.com
,
Maxim
,
RaceCar Engineering
,
AutoWeek
,
Virginia Living
, and others.
Dan has authored books on the
Honda S2000
and
Dodge Viper
sports cars and contributed automotive content to the consumer finance book,
Fight For Your Money
.
He is a member and past president of the
Washington Automotive Press Association
and is a member of the
Society of Automotive Engineers
.

title: Exploring BMW Group's Advances in AI-Driven Battery Production
content:
EV Batteries
Lithium-Ion Batteries
Battery Manufacturing
Industry Trends
Exploring BMW Group's Advances in AI-Driven Battery Production
Explore BMW Group's slideshow on AI-driven battery cell production at Battery Cell Competence Centers, showcasing innovation and sustainability.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 13, 2026
8 Slides
Start slideshow
BMW Group project applies artificial intelligence (AI) to optimize the entire battery cell production process, including electrode manufacturing.
Courtesy of BMW Group.
The
BMW Group
is inviting individuals to explore its latest developments in battery cell production through a slideshow presentation titled
"BMW Group and University of Zagreb: Advances in Battery Cell Production Using Artificial Intelligence."
This presentation highlights the collaboration between the BMW Group and the University of Zagreb’s Regional Centre of Excellence for Robotic Technology (CRTA) in advancing battery cell manufacturing processes.
The slideshow focuses on the
“Insight”
research project, a pilot initiative at the Battery Cell Competence Center (BCCC) in Munich. This project applies artificial intelligence (AI) to optimize the entire battery cell production process, including electrode manufacturing, end-of-line testing, and direct recycling. By utilizing AI-driven models, the project aims to reduce testing efforts, raw material usage, and production costs while maintaining high-quality standards.
Related:
Upstream Monitoring Redefines EV Battery Quality Control
The BCCC in Munich, along with facilities in Parsdorf and Salching, plays a key role in the BMW Group’s efforts to innovate in battery technology. The
“Insight”
project uses real-time production data and historical test results to predict process parameters and performance outcomes. This approach has led to a significant reduction in material and time requirements for individual production steps, with savings exceeding 50%.
This collaboration reflects the BMW Group’s focus on integrating advanced technologies into its production processes to support sustainability and the future of mobility. By combining theoretical research with practical applications, the partnership with the University of Zagreb demonstrates a commitment to improving efficiency and reducing environmental impact in battery cell production.
Click through to see the slideshow, which offers an opportunity to learn more about these AI advancements and their potential impact on the battery industry.
Start slideshow
About the Author
Maria Guerra
Senior Editor-Battery Technology, Informa Markets
Maria L. Guerra is a Senior Editor at
Battery Technology
, blending her engineering background with a passion for technology journalism. She holds a Bachelor of Science in Electrical Engineering from Universidad Metropolitana in Caracas, Venezuela, and a Master of Science in Electrical Engineering from NYU Tandon School of Engineering.
Guerra's career spans power systems engineering, oil & gas consulting, and technical writing and editing. She worked for several years at Kellogg, Brown & Root Ltd. (KBR) in the UK, conducting power system studies and providing technical support for offshore and onshore projects. During her internship at Pequiven S.A. in Venezuela, she trained neural networks using MATLAB (Kohonen Map) to estimate electrical circuit variables for oil wells (e.g., voltage, current, and power).
In addition to her engineering roles, Guerra worked as a freelance technical editor, refining complex technical content for clarity and accuracy. She later became a Power/Analog Editor at
Electronic Design
, covering advancements in the semiconductor market, focusing on power/analog electronics systems.
At
Battery Technology
, Guerra now delivers in-depth coverage of battery manufacturing, EV advancements, energy storage systems, and the evolving landscape of critical minerals and second-life batteries. She is passionate about uncovering the stories that shape the future of electrification, from cutting-edge battery innovations to policy shifts driving sustainable energy.
Guerra is also a member of
The Battery Show North America Advisory Board
, where she contributes her expertise to advising on industry trends and speaker selection for The Battery Show North America, while helping to shape discussions on battery technology and electrification. She was part of the award-winning Battery Technology team recognized with a
Neal Award for Best Coverage of the 2024 Election
by the Software and Information Industry Association (SIIA). Guerra received an individual
Neal Award for Best Explanatory Content
in 2026 for her coverage of battery breakthroughs.
Maria Guerra is based in Brooklyn, NY, and can be reached at

title: SoftBank’s Zinc-Halogen Batteries Challenge LFP in Data Centers
content:
Battery Applications
Materials
Industry Outlook
Industry Trends
SoftBank’s Zinc-Halogen Batteries Challenge LFP in AI Data Centers
Discover why SoftBank is betting on zinc-halogen battery technology for safer energy storage in AI data centers, in collaboration with DeltaX and COSMOS LAB.
Maria Guerra
,
Senior Editor-Battery Technology
,
Informa Markets
May 12, 2026
4 Min Read
SoftBank is developing zinc-halogen batteries to power Data Centers.
PhonlamaiPhoto/iStock / Getty Images Plus
SoftBank Corp. announced the launch of a new battery business in Japan, aimed at building next-generation power infrastructure to meet the surging electricity demand driven by AI adoption. This initiative comes at a key moment, as
AI hyperscale is driving a shift
in energy storage for data centers. As AI workloads create unpredictable power demands, battery systems are evolving from mere backup solutions to active infrastructure, reshaping how energy is stored and used.
In collaboration with
COSMOS LAB
and DeltaX Co., Ltd., SoftBank is
developing
zinc-halogen batteries that utilize pure water as the electrolyte, eliminating fire risks associated with lithium-ion batteries.  To maximize the performance of each individual battery cell, SoftBank will also collaborate with DeltaX's to develop energy storage systems using DeltaX's Cell Connecting System (CCS) design and
Cell to Pack (CTP) technology
. According to the statement, “This is the world's first product among commercially deployed aqueous zinc–halogen battery cells to achieve energy density comparable to lithium-ion batteries, as of May 11, 2026, according to SoftBank's research.”
Related:
Battery Swapping Products Drive Innovation in Construction
With plans to achieve mass production by FY2027 and scale to gigawatt-hour levels by FY2028, SoftBank aims to deploy these batteries in its AI Data Center in Osaka and expand their use to grid applications, industrial facilities, and residential systems. This initiative not only addresses Japan’s growing energy needs but also positions zinc-halogen batteries as a key player in the global energy transition.
Image of Innovative Battery Cell. Courtesy of SoftBank.
What are zinc-halogen batteries?
Zinc-halogen batteries are rechargeable batteries that use zinc and a halogen element—typically bromine—as the active materials. These batteries belong to the category of flow batteries, which store energy in liquid electrolytes that are circulated through the system during operation. The key components of a zinc-halogen battery include a zinc electrode, a halogen-based electrolyte, a separator or ion-exchange membrane, and an electrolyte flow system.
During charging, electrical energy is used to deposit zinc on the anode while converting bromine to bromide ions in the electrolyte. When the battery discharges, the zinc dissolves back into the electrolyte, and bromide ions are converted back into bromine, releasing electrical energy. This reversible chemical reaction forms the basis of the battery's operation.
Zinc-halogen batteries vs. LFP batteries
Lithium iron phosphate (LFP) batteries
are currently one of the most common choices for stationary energy storage systems, but zinc-halogen batteries offer several distinct advantages and trade-offs when compared to LFP technology.
Related:
From Apollo to Artemis II: Batteries in Space!
1. Safety
: Zinc-halogen batteries are inherently safer than LFP batteries. They use an aqueous electrolyte (pure water), which eliminates the risk of fire or thermal runaway. In contrast, LFP batteries, while safer than other lithium-ion chemistries, still rely on flammable organic electrolytes, which pose a fire risk under extreme conditions.
2. Energy Density
: LFP batteries generally have a higher energy density than zinc-halogen batteries, making them more compact and suitable for applications where space is limited. However, zinc-halogen batteries are competitive in energy density for stationary applications and offer the added benefit of scalability by increasing the size of the electrolyte tanks.
3.
Cycle Life and Durability
: Both zinc-halogen and LFP batteries have long cycle lives, but zinc-halogen batteries excel in durability for large-scale, long-duration energy storage. The flow battery design allows for easier maintenance and replacement of components, whereas LFP batteries may experience capacity fade over time due to electrode degradation.
Related:
Understanding High Impedance and Its Risks for Pacemakers
4. Cost and Resource Availability
: Zinc-halogen batteries use abundant and locally available materials like zinc and halogen, which can reduce supply chain risks and costs, especially in regions like Japan. LFP batteries, on the other hand, rely on lithium and other critical minerals, which are subject to supply chain constraints and price volatility. And in the case of SoftBank, key raw materials, such as halogen and zinc, can be procured locally in Japan, helping to strengthen the supply chain.
5. Environmental Impact
: Zinc-halogen batteries have a lower environmental impact due to their use of non-toxic, recyclable materials. LFP batteries, while less harmful than other lithium-ion chemistries, still involve mining and processing of lithium, which has significant environmental consequences.
Morrow LFP battery cells. Courtesy of Morrow Batteries.
While LFP batteries remain a dominant choice for stationary energy storage, zinc-halogen batteries offer a compelling alternative, particularly for large-scale applications where safety, scalability, and resource availability are critical. SoftBank’s investment in zinc-halogen technology highlights its potential to address the growing energy demands of AI-driven infrastructure while overcoming the limitations of existing battery technologies.
However, zinc-halogen batteries are not without challenges. Managing the corrosion caused by elemental bromine remains a significant technical hurdle, requiring specialized materials and system designs to ensure long-term reliability. Despite this, zinc-halogen batteries could play a pivotal role in building a safer, more sustainable, and scalable energy infrastructure in the AI-driven era.

title: Mastering EV Battery Diagnostics for the Next Service Era
content:
EV Batteries
Testing & Safety
Design & Manufacturing
Industry Trends
Mastering EV Battery Diagnostics for the Next Service Era
Discover how advanced EV battery diagnostics and purpose-built systems can transform service efficiency, sustainability, and repair precision.
Kevin Poole
,
Senior Director of Engineering
,
EV Battery Solutions by Cox Automotive
May 12, 2026
5 Min Read
Engineer using a General Use Module Tester, known as GUMTer.
Courtesy of EV Battery Solutions by Cox Automotive.
As electric vehicles (EVs) move further into the mainstream, the industry’s service challenge is becoming more complex. Selling and deploying EVs is only one part of the equation. Keeping them on the road safely, affordably, and efficiently will require a more mature approach to battery diagnostics.
For service providers, original equipment manufacturers (OEMs), dealers, and fleet operators, one of the most important questions is no longer simply whether an
EV battery
works. The more useful question is: what exactly is happening inside the pack, and what is the most appropriate next step?
The importance of module-level diagnostics
Not every EV battery concern requires a full pack replacement. In many cases, the issue may be isolated to a specific module or component, or to a performance imbalance. A battery may contain modules that are outside specification, while other modules remain usable. Without the right diagnostic process, service teams may be forced to perform broader repairs than necessary, increasing costs, downtime, and waste.
Related:
Upstream Monitoring Redefines EV Battery Quality Control
That is why module-level diagnostics are becoming increasingly important.
EV battery packs vary widely across manufacturers, platforms, and chemistries. Battery pack layouts,
battery cooling systems
, access points, connectors, voltage ranges, and service requirements can differ significantly. That variation makes it difficult to rely on a single generic process or assume that pack-level information alone will provide enough insight.
A more effective diagnostic approach starts by narrowing the problem. Which module is underperforming? Is the issue related to capacity, internal resistance, imbalance, leakage, or another failure mode? Can the module be safely reused? Does it need to be balanced, discharged, repaired, or replaced? And can those decisions be made in a way that is consistent, traceable, and safe?
Those questions matter because battery repair is not only a technical issue. It is also an operational issue. A repair process must support technician safety, protect the customer experience, meet OEM standards, and move at a pace that makes sense for real-world service volumes. Better diagnostics can help reduce unnecessary teardown, limit handling risk, and improve confidence in the repair path.
General Use Module Tester, known as GUMTer. Courtesy of EV Battery Solutions by Cox Automotive.
Tools and processes for sustainable battery service
At
EV Battery Solutions by Cox Automotive
, this need led to the development of the General Use Module Tester, known as GUMTer. The tool was created to help engineers and technicians evaluate EV battery modules with greater precision, including capacity measurement, pulse testing, module balancing, and controlled discharge. While the tool itself is proprietary, the broader need it addresses is one the entire industry is facing: EV battery service requires better data, better process control, and more repeatable decision-making.
Related:
Subaru’s 2026 Solterra EV Touring XT Delivers Power, Range, & Mixed Ride Quality
Module-level testing can also support more sustainable battery service. When a pack is treated as a single pass-or-fail unit, usable components may be removed from service prematurely. By identifying which modules still meet defined standards, service teams can preserve more of the value already built into the battery. That can help extend battery life, reduce demand for new replacement packs, and keep valuable materials in use longer.
Platforms like GUMTer help evaluate module performance and provide information that can guide repair decisions. In some applications, higher-current testing can also help better reflect real-world load demand. Controlled discharge capabilities can bring modules to safer levels before additional handling or downstream processing.
Key questions for effective EV battery diagnostics
The most important takeaway is not that one tool solves every diagnostic challenge. It is that the next phase of EV battery service will require purpose-built systems and processes that reflect the complexity of the battery itself. Service teams preparing for higher EV volumes should consider several practical questions:
Related:
EV Supply Chain Needs Strong Government Support
Are diagnostic processes designed to identify the root cause, or only confirm that a battery is not performing as expected?
Can testing be performed at the appropriate level: pack, module or component, to support a targeted repair decision?
Is the data captured in a way that supports traceability, warranty decisions, quality control, and future process improvement?
Are technicians equipped not only with high-voltage safety training, but also with clear workflows that reduce unnecessary handling and improve consistency?
Can the process scale as EV service demand grows?
EV battery assembly. Courtesy of PhonlamaiPhoto / iStock via Getty Images.
These questions will become more important as EVs age, and more batteries move beyond the earliest phases of ownership. The industry will need service models that help determine when to repair, when to replace, when to reuse and when to recycle. That requires more than replacement parts. It requires diagnostic discipline.
For OEMs and dealers, better diagnostics can help reduce unnecessary warranty cost, improve turnaround time and support stronger customer confidence. For fleets, it can mean less downtime and more predictable maintenance planning. For the broader EV ecosystem, it can help protect battery value across the lifecycle.
The future of EV battery service will likely be defined by teams that can combine engineering expertise, safe handling practices, reliable data and repeatable processes. Tools like GUMTer show one direction that future is heading: toward smarter, more targeted diagnostics that help service teams make better decisions before reaching for a replacement pack.
As EV adoption grows, the industry’s ability to support batteries after the sale will become a key measure of maturity. The next era of battery diagnostics will not be about simply finding failures. It will be about understanding them well enough to make the right repair decision, protect the value of the battery and keep more EVs on the road.
About the Author
Kevin Poole
Senior Director of Engineering , EV Battery Solutions by Cox Automotive
Kevin Poole
is the Senior Director of Engineering for EV Battery Solutions by Cox Automotive, bringing more than 25 years of experience in engineering, quality, operations, and innovation leadership. Over the course of his career, he has led cross-functional teams, developed advanced product strategies, secured major business wins, and driven operational and quality improvements across the automotive and manufacturing sectors. He holds both bachelor’s and master’s degrees in mechanical engineering from Lawrence Technological University and is a Six Sigma Green Belt with multiple automotive patents.

Kevin Poole

title: Reinventing Thermal Battery Protection Systems for E-Aviation
content:
Automotive & Mobility
Lithium-Ion Batteries
Materials
Thermal Management
Industry Trends
Reinventing Thermal Battery Protection Systems for Electric Aviation
Electric aircraft are transforming aviation with innovative battery thermal protection systems, ensuring safety, efficiency, and lightweight design for the future of flight.
Tim Burbey
,
President and Co-Founder
,
Blueshift
May 11, 2026
6 Min Read
Electric aircraft are transforming aviation with innovative battery thermal protection systems.
mikkelwilliam/iStock / Getty Images Plus.
One aerospace principle has and always will be a constant: weight is the enemy. That has never been more relevant than today, as aircraft design goes through its most significant revolution in a generation. Legacy manufacturers and startups alike are investing heavily in vehicles that fly at hypersonic speeds, run on hydrogen, or are powered by electric battery systems — all while pushing to replace traditional materials with lighter alternatives. These new designs create unprecedented thermal challenges that demand a step change in materials technology. Nowhere is this more acute than in battery-powered aircraft.
The 787: A warning about weight
Even on conventional combustion aircraft, batteries are becoming more critical. The Boeing 787 Dreamliner uses lithium-ion batteries to start its auxiliary power unit, back up critical systems in the event of generator failure, and power flight-control electronics. Boeing chose lithium-ion batteries over the older nickel-cadmium batteries used on the 777 and 747 because lithium-ion can deliver a large burst of power quickly — exactly what's needed to start a jet engine.
Related:
Lexus Engineers Tackle Challenges of Hybrid and EV Design for ES Sedan
In January 2013, two 787s suffered battery fires within days of each other — one in the air over Japan, and one on the ground in Boston. All 51 delivered aircraft were grounded for four months. Boeing's fix involved switching from an aluminum battery enclosure to a stainless steel box, adding ceramic thermal shielding between cells, and installing new ductwork to vent fumes overboard. The result added approximately 68 kg to the aircraft — enough to completely cancel out the weight savings that had made lithium-ion attractive in the first place. It was an expensive lesson in what happens when thermal protection is treated as an afterthought.
787 Commercial airplane in a hangar for maintenance. Courtesy of Martin Chavez/ iStock / Getty Images Plus.
Lilium's legacy in battery innovation for electric aviation
Fast forward to 2024, and electric aircraft company Lilium was assembling its all-electric jet outside Munich at Oberpfaffenhofen Airport. Where the 787's battery system stored roughly 2.8 kWh to support the aircraft, Lilium's battery system targeted 62.5 kWh — more than 20 times the energy — and served as the sole source of propulsion. The 787's batteries are roughly the size of a car battery, delivering emergency power for seconds at a time. Lilium's battery system was delivering a megawatt of power on demand, cycling hard on every single flight.
Lilium's approach to thermal protection reflected an entirely different set of demands. Rather than heavy metal enclosures, Lilium used lightweight composite battery casings and distributed risk across 10 independently functioning battery packs, designed so that the failure of any single pack could not bring down the aircraft. It was a fundamentally more elegant architecture than Boeing's containment-box approach and, critically, achieved that safety level without the weight penalty. Lilium was unable to secure the capital needed to reach commercial service and ceased operations in early 2025.
Related:
Ford Unveils New Details on the Universal EV Platform and Pickup
But Lilium’s innovative approach to battery architecture and thermal protection set a benchmark for electric aviation, demonstrating the potential for lightweight, efficient designs. Building on this foundation, the FAA’s eIPP program seeks to accelerate the integration of such cutting-edge technologies into urban air mobility and other advanced applications.
Industry leaders join FAA's groundbreaking pilot program
In March 2026, U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy and the FAA
announced eight projects
selected under the Advanced Air Mobility and eVTOL Integration Pilot Program (eIPP). Spanning 26 states, these projects focus on urban air taxi services, regional passenger transport, cargo logistics, emergency medical response, and autonomous operations. Key industry players participating in the program include Joby Aviation, Wisk Aero, Archer Aviation, and Beta Technologies.
Joby
is targeting the launch of passenger services in Dubai later this year. Flights under the eIPP are set to begin this summer, with cargo operations expected to lead due to simpler liability concerns, less congested airspace, and the fact that paying passenger services in U.S. urban airspace remain at least a year away.
Related:
Bosch Advances EV Motor Efficiency With AI & Cooling Tech While Reducing Rare-Earth Material Use
Joby Aviation's eVTOL aircraft in cruise mode. Courtesy of Joby Aviation.
Why automotive solutions don't work in the air
In the very early days of electric aircraft development, the default assumption was to apply the same thermal runaway management approaches used in electric vehicles (EVs). It quickly became clear that this was the wrong starting point.
The distinction comes down to consequences. When an EV battery goes into
thermal runaway
, you pull over, get out, and call 911. When an aircraft battery does the same, there is nowhere to go. Automotive thermal protection standards are essentially egress standards, where the goal is to buy the occupants enough time to escape. Heavy steel enclosures, thick ceramic shields, and dense insulation are all acceptable solutions when you're already moving a two-ton vehicle down a road.
In aviation, every gram of thermal protection material is a gram taken from battery energy, range, or payload. An electric aircraft engineered to automotive containment standards may simply be too heavy to fly commercially, or even at all. Beyond weight, the problem is structurally different: an aircraft battery must keep the vehicle airborne during a thermal event, not merely slow a fire long enough to open a door. It must do so at altitude, under vibration, and with reduced atmospheric pressure, which changes how gases vent and how fires propagate.
The result is a completely different design problem, requiring thin, light, thermally capable materials rather than the brute-force containment hardware that works perfectly well in a parking lot.
An unexpected gift to conventional aviation
Here is where the story takes an ironic turn. The brutal material requirements imposed by electric aircraft — where there is simply no room for heavy conventional solutions — have forced a new generation of ultra-light, high-performance thermal protection systems and flame barriers into existence. These are material combinations that either didn't exist commercially before or hadn't been considered for aerospace applications.
And now those innovations are flowing back upstream. Traditional aerospace manufacturers, who have relied on combustion engines and legacy materials for decades, are finding that the thermal protection systems and flame barriers developed under pressure for electric aviation outperform what they had been using all along, and at a fraction of the weight.
Managing transient thermal events — whether from flames, fires, battery failures, or aerodynamic heating — will always be fundamental to aircraft battery design. The pioneers building tomorrow's electric aircraft batteries are inadvertently solving yesterday's problems, giving the entire aerospace industry access to materials that are thinner, lighter, faster to install, and more thermally capable than anything that emerged from the conventional development program.
About the Author
Tim Burbey
President and Co-Founder , Blueshift
Tim Burbey is President and Co-Founder of Blueshift, with more than 30 years of experience in materials science. He leads the company’s work in advanced thermal protection systems for aerospace, medical devices, electronics, and other mission-critical applications.

title: Europe's Battery Manufacturing Scale-Up: Key Challenges Ahead
content:
Battery Manufacturing
Market Analysis
News
Battery Show Europe Delves Into EU Efforts to Scale Up Manufacturing
The Battery Show Europe explores critical challenges facing EU battery manufacturing scale-up, including supply chain dependency, workforce development, and cost competitiveness.
Spencer Chin
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 7, 2026
3 Min Read
The challenges facing Europe in scaling up manufacturing to stay competitive will be the focus of several Battery Show Europe sessions.
Battery Show Europe
At The Battery Show Europe June 9 through 11 in Stuttgart, Germany, there will be several sessions examining the issue Europe faces in scaling up manufacturing to meet future battery needs for EVs as well as industrial applications. Key concerns include current European reliance on imported supply chains, the ability to develop a skilled workforce, achieving suitable production yields, and more.
Here’s a glimpse at some of the key sessions:
From Supplier Dependency to Internal Expertise: Building European Battery Cell Capability
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=7
Tuesday, June 9
3:00 PM - 3:30 PM CET
As Europe accelerates battery cell manufacturing, the ability to develop internal expertise, rather than relying solely on supplier-provided training, will define long-term operational success. This session explores proven strategies to build a skilled workforce, transfer critical knowledge, and avoid the pitfalls of supplier knowledge dependency.
Building Europe’s Battery Manufacturing Capability to Move Beyond Import Dependence
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=221
Tuesday, June 9
2:00 PM - 2:30 PM CET
As battery cells become one of the most critical inputs into almost everything we rely on, Europe’s total reliance on imported supply chains is increasingly seen as a structural risk. Geopolitical volatility, long lead times and limited supply chain proximity are driving growing demand for locally produced cells, not purely on cost grounds, but for resilience, responsiveness and long-term industrial security. This session explores what it actually takes to build credible manufacturing capability, and why a more phased, de-risked approach focused on knowledge transfer, targeted demand and operational focus may offer a more realistic and faster route to scale.
Team Design & Manufacturing - How DfM can improve the current situation of EU cell production?
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=36
Tuesday, June 9
3:30 PM - 3:45 PM CET
European battery cell production faces considerable challenges, including cost pressures, equipment complexities, and difficult ramping up to target yield. A significant improvement can be achieved by integrating Design for Manufacturing (DfM) methodologies, ensuring that design and manufacturing teams collaborate closely from the earliest development phases.By applying DfM principles, cell designs can directly address manufacturing constraints, significantly increasing production success rates and mitigating risks related to equipment ordering, machine design, and operational implementation.
Strengthening the European Battery Industry: The Reality of Competing at Giga-Scale
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=211
Tuesday, June 9
3:30 PM - 3:45 PM CET
This session sets out the industrial realities behind Europe’s battery ambitions. It focuses on the practical conditions required to build a viable European battery value chain, including cost competitiveness, scale, speed of decision-making and the consequences of continued policy uncertainty. Rather than revisiting technology narratives, the session examines whether Europe’s current approach can deliver industrial outcomes at the pace and scale required.
Battery Manufacturing in Transition: Policy, Cost Dynamics and Strategic Positioning
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=59
Tuesday, June 9
11:00 AM - 11:30 AM CET
As competition for battery production intensifies, policy measures such as subsidies, tariffs, local content requirements, and export controls are increasingly shaping investment decisions, cost structures, and trade flows. Policy shifts, economic and supply chain risks are redefining industrial strategies and reshaping global supply chains. This session will provide an analytical overview of market trends and competitiveness in the battery and electric vehicle sectors, examining how regulatory levers, technology choices, and supply chain constraints are influencing regional positions.
Cost Competitiveness and Europe’s Industrial Scale-Up Challenge
https://tbse26.mapyourshow.com/8_0/sessions/session-details.cfm?scheduleid=172
Tuesday, June 9
9:30 AM - 10:15 AM CET
Europe’s battery industry faces an ongoing competitiveness challenge. Manufacturing costs remain high, ramp-up excellence is an existential challenge, and market demand and policy signals remain uncertain. Despite strong engineering capability and safety leadership, Europe is still challenged to translate investment into cost-competitive, high-volume production.
This keynote panel examines why ramp-up execution is a critical challenge for Europe, and how an entire battery ecosystem is required to be competitive. Bringing together senior leaders from across the value chain, the discussion will focus on what is needed to successfully scale up battery production in Europe and what the pitfalls are in the transition from pilot to industrial production.
The Battery Show Europe | Electric & Hybrid Vehicle Technology Expo Europe
Jun 9, 2026 TO Jun 11, 2026
|
Messe Stuttgart | Stuttgart, Germany
Join the leading meeting place for the advanced battery and H/EV technology community. Meet manufacturers, suppliers, engineers, thought leaders and decision-makers for a conference and trade fair focused on the latest developments in the advanced battery and automotive industries.

At the same time, Spencer has adapted his reporting and editing style to the digital age. It's not just news stories and features but also social media posts, and videos, as seen below. Adapting to 21st-century media consumption habits has kept Spencer on his toes learning new tools and skills, though he admits it is sometimes hard to teach an old dog new tricks.

title: Ford Universal EV Platform Details
content:
Automotive & Mobility
Design & Manufacturing
News
Ford Unveils New Details on the Universal EV Platform and Pickup
Ford's new EV platform integrates advanced aerodynamics, lightweight materials, and innovative battery tech to redefine electric pickups.
Dan Carney
,
Senior Editor
,
Design News
May 6, 2026
8 Min Read
A graphical representation of airflow over the upcoming electric midsize Ford pickup reveals hints of the vehicle's profile.
Ford Motor Co.
At a Glance
Ford's EV platform cuts parts by 25 percent
Aerodynamics boost range by 15 percent
Battery tech enables affordable home power backup
Ford
has raised the curtain just a bit more on its upcoming
Universal EV Platform
and the
mid-sized battery-electric pickup truck
that will be the first example, with more details on the platform’s technology and a peek behind the scenes at the integrated technical center in Long Beach, CA, where cross-functional teams have developed the design.
The one-time secretive “skunkworks” team is responsible for all aspects of vehicle development from design to prototyping to testing. The facility is called the Electric Vehicle Development Center (EVDC), and it includes two buildings encompassing 250,000 square feet of floor space.
The Ford Electric Vehicle Development Center in Long Beach, CA. FORD
Having all the capabilities needed to design, prototype, and test EVs on site accelerates the process, according to Nathan Kollross, senior operations manager. “It is all 100 feet, 200 feet away to get your hands on a part,” he said. “Having the team all in one space and seeing how quickly and efficiently we can work together is the biggest positive.”
That team is now 350 strong, with designers, engineers, and supply chain experts all represented in the effort to rethink how Ford builds cars using the Ford Universal EV Production System.
The in-person team in Long Beach has access to a meeting room with a floor-to-ceiling LED wall that not only displays life-sized vehicles but can be used to let them collaborate with another 500 colleagues in Dearborn, Palo Alto, or at the Louisville factory where the pickup will be made.
Ford engineers have provided some more detail on the truck’s specifications, filling in some more blanks beyond the use of large-scale castings [which Ford terms “unicastings”], zonal electronic controllers, and low-cost lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) batteries disclosed previously.
For example, Ford says that the front and rear castings are 27% lighter than those already in use on other vehicles and that the front and rear cast structures serve in place of 146 discrete parts on today’s Maverick mid-size pickup.
“Using unicastings enables 25% fewer fasteners than a typical program,” stated Vlad Bogachuk, vehicle structure and CAE leader in Ford’s YouTube video about the vehicle. “With the reduction in parts comes a reduction in joints and sealing. Therefore, the robots in the factory are giving us measurable gains in product quality and throughput.”
At the same time, the zonal architecture slashes the need for electronic control units from 30 to five, reported Luccas De Tullio, who is responsible for power electronics firmware and controls.
“We're also moving from a 12-volt system to a 48-volt system, which allows us to reduce the copper thickness of the harness,” he said. “The DC-to-DC converter, which converts the 400 volts battery power to 48 volts, now shares a board and components with the AC/DC charger.”
Integrating those components provides additional benefits.  “This single module also manages power distribution, battery management, and provides AC power back to your home during an outage,” he said. “Grouping these together allowed us to consolidate common parts such as sensors and auxiliary supplies, reducing the number of components and printed circuit boards.”
That detail about the vehicle sending AC power to the home is significant. The few EVs today that are capable of bi-directional charging to power owners’ homes send DC to the house. That requires homeowners to buy costly inverters for their homes to provide the AC current the house needs. The parts and labor for such systems cost $12,000 to $15,000, making the notion of powering the home from the car decidedly less attractive.
The Ford Universal EV, in contrast, will send DC to the home, and charging station manufacturers like ChargePoint say that their upcoming bidirectional home chargers will be able to route this back into the house with no need for costly inverters.
The Universal EV’s on-board diagnostics system will maximize battery cell life through careful management, said De Tullio. “We're leveraging machine learning to create a predictive model for the battery pack, which will track and adapt its usage based on both historical data and real-world conditions. This will, for example, allow us to adjust charging rates to help prevent degradation of the battery over time.”
That battery will use prismatic cells, even though the industry seems to be shifting away from those toward the cylindrical cells Tesla likes. Alan Clarke, universal EV platform and product leader, asserts that the prismatic cells will permit structural efficiency. “Our prismatic LFP form factor enables an incredibly efficient cell-to-structure architecture.”
“The battery pack also uses a multi-layer single-piece flexible circuit board,” he added. “This board integrates low-voltage, high-voltage, and thermal sensing connections, reducing hundreds of bus bars and wires into a single part. We can measure voltage and temperature and connect each cell using this hyper-efficient module. Overall, the structure architecture reduces non-cell cost.”
This works with Ford’s in-house-designed power electronics that squeeze inside the battery pack. “This eliminates extra connectors, enclosures, mass, and overall volume,” said Clarke. “Instead of carrying the cells, this new architecture couples the cells directly to the structure.”
A Ford engineer loads a lithium-iron-phosphate prismatic battery cell into the test cabinet. FORD
All of this power and its judicious management can be for naught if the vehicle has brick-like aerodynamics that waste the energy shoving the air aside, said aerodynamicist Saleem Merkt. The team wrung drag from every possible source, producing a design that he says is 15% more aerodynamically efficient than any pickup on the road today.
Aero efficiency is 15% better than any truck on the market today. That corresponds to an 15% driving range, or 50 miles, he said. The advantage is even bigger at highway speed, when the new Ford’s design is worth an additional 30%.
To achieve this, the aerodynamic team attacked every possible source of drag.  “We streamlined the underbody to manage the air. We painstakingly designed underneath the car to steer the front tire wakes directly onto the rear tires, hiding them from the high-speed flow and ultimately reducing drag,” Merkt said. “Changes like this can contribute to an estimated 4.5 more miles of range for the customer.”
An EVDC metrology engineer examines a part. FORD
There was more at the rear of the truck.  “We position the drive units as low as possible to reduce the angle of the half shafts, making them last longer and minimizing wasted energy caused by friction in the joints. We then sculpted the motor case to allow the air we carefully managed at the front to pass under the vehicle uninterrupted to reduce the size of the wake.”
Obviously, with a pickup, we think of the upright grille and cab as the primary culprits when it comes to drag, and in the teaser image, we can see a sleeker front end reminiscent of Ford’s pickups from the ‘90s jellybean design era. But the roof is also important for steering airflow to the bed. “The roof was sculpted to carefully shed the high-speed air on top and a teardrop shape over the bed, creating a virtual surface for the air to completely skip over it,” he said. “To the air, it's no longer a truck.”
Expensive EVs propose to reduce the drag from side-view mirrors by replacing them with lipstick-sized cameras and display screens in the cabin. Ford decided to just work on the mirrors instead. “Instead of separate motors for glass adjustment and another motor for folding, we've merged those functions into a single actuator,” Merkt explained.
“The entire body of the mirror now moves,” he continued. “The mirror body size is now more than 20% smaller than a conventional mirror, which reduces mass, saves on cost, and unlocks a more aerodynamic shape, resulting in 1.5 miles more range for the vehicle.”
Welding components on-site in the EVDC's fabrication shop speeds the development process. FORD
To accomplish these achievements, the EVDC team was provided with an engineer’s dream in terms of equipment. The center includes seven CNC milling towers for modeling at scale, 3D printers, a trim shop, and a 360-degree turntable.
The fabrication shop can deliver full-size prototypes in as little as 30 minutes (though some do take a few days), so the team can iterate more quickly. “Our absolute deadline might not change,” said Daniel Smith, director of occupant architecture and seating. “But we can do three or four more iterations and try more variety, while taking more risks to try crazy ideas in-house.”
The 5-axis Fooke 911 gantry machine with a large robotic arm can carve full-size vehicle models, and water jet cutting tools can also cut metal. The trim shop’s sewing and cutting machines can prototype for seating and interior elements. That means that “we can all poke and prod and determine what’s good, bad, or absolutely fantastic,” said Scott Anderson, senior manager of seating.
Ford EVDC engineer testing high-voltage circuits. FORD
The most important component to poke and prod in an EV is the battery, so the EVDC has a comprehensive battery testing facility on site. The lab’s thermal shock chamber tests battery packs with an ultra-high precision coulometer technology that simulates years of wear in weeks.
The thermal lab can house a full-size vehicle bake and freeze batteries to see how the other parts hold up. The climatic chassis dyno lab can simulate wind resistance, speed, humidity, and grade on a dynamometer that has 48-inch rollers.
“Testing early, fail fast. That is one of our commandments,” said Akshaya Srinivasan, director of range, performance, and battery systems modeling. “We target zero failures, so we have to understand where they failed. Our goal is to build and design the system that rarely, if ever, fails in our customers' hands.”
About the Author
Dan Carney
Senior Editor, Design News
Dan’s coverage of the auto industry over three decades has taken him to the
racetracks
, automotive engineering centers,
vehicle simulators
, wind tunnels, and crash-test labs of the world.
A member of the

, Dan also
contributes car reviews to

magazine, serves on the

jury, and has judged the collegiate

competition.
Dan is a winner of the

for automotive writing, as well as the

Washington Automotive Press Association's Golden Quill award
.
Dan, testing the 2025 Aston Martin Vanquish. ANDY MORGAN/ASTON MARTIN
He has held a
Sports Car Club of America
racing license since 1991, is an SCCA National race winner, two-time SCCA Runoffs competitor in
Formula F
, and an
Old Dominion Region
Driver of the Year award winner. Co-drove a Ford Focus 1.0-liter EcoBoost to
16 Federation Internationale de l’Automobile-accredited world speed records
over distances from just under 1km to over 4,104km at the CERAM test circuit in Mortefontaine, France.
He was also a longtime contributor to the Society of Automotive Engineers'
Automotive Engineering International
magazine.
He specializes in analyzing technical developments, particularly in the areas of motorsports, efficiency, and safety.
He has been published in
The New York Times

Dan has authored books on the
Honda S2000
and
Dodge Viper
sports cars and contributed automotive content to the consumer finance book,
Fight For Your Money
.
He is a member and past president of the
Washington Automotive Press Association
and is a member of the
Society of Automotive Engineers

title: Google, Kaiser Permanente, Urban Outfitters Leaders Join GS1
content:
Article
Supply Chain Management (SCM)
Google, Kaiser Permanente, Urban Outfitters Leaders Join GS1
By
Aaron McMillan
May 26, 2026
undefined mins
Share
Share
Senthil Subramanian, Principal Engineer and Area Technical Lead, Google
Executives from Google, Kaiser Permanente and Urban Outfitters join the GS1 US board to drive digital transformation and supply chain efficiency
The GS1 US Board of Governors has elected three new members to join the GS1 US Board: Senthil Subramanian, Principal Engineer and Area Technical Lead at
Google
, Steven Chyung, Senior Vice President, Chief Supply Chain and Procurement Officer at Kaiser Permanente and Omar A. Tovar, Chief Logistics Officer at Urban Outfitters.
GS1 US enables companies to power their supply chains to deliver safe, consistent, authentic and trusted experiences. Best known as a source for UPC barcodes, GS1 is a not-for-profit, global data standards organisation that creates a common language for companies to identify, capture and share trusted data that links their physical and digital supply chains.
Millions of businesses around the world power commerce with GS1 Standards.
These executives join a distinguished group of leaders shaping the GS1 US strategy. Together, they drive the adoption of GS1 Standards as a universal foundation for supply chain efficiency, retail commerce and enhanced consumer and patient experiences. The cross-industry Board of Governors spans key sectors, including apparel, general merchandise, grocery, foodservice and healthcare.
Industry leaders driving data strategy
"At a time when supply chains are being reshaped by
digital transformation
and rising complexity, these leaders bring invaluable perspective to help industry advance large-scale data management, supply chain operations and global logistics," says Bob Carpenter, President and CEO of GS1 US.
"Their collective experience will help guide our work as shared and standardised data becomes even more central to how modern supply chains function and scale."
Bob Carpenter, President and CEO of GS1 US
Senthil oversees the Shopping Graph at Google, ensuring the integrity, coverage, richness and freshness of the massive product datasets that power global shopping experiences across Search, YouTube and Ads.
An expert in
large-scale data infrastructure
, Senthil previously served as Vice President at Yahoo, leading advertising infrastructure and targeting systems.
He was also a founding member of the original Hadoop team, helping pioneer open-source big data.
Steven Chyung, Senior Vice President, Chief Supply Chain and Procurement Officer, Kaiser Permanente
Steven leads Kaiser Permanente’s end-to-end supply chain organisation, directing sourcing, procurement, fulfilment and demand forecasting to ensure reliable, cost-effective service delivery.
He joined Kaiser Permanente from SCL Health, where he built and managed shared services across
supply chain
, clinical engineering and real estate.
Previously, Steven held leadership roles at Novartis in both Europe and the US, establishing global Centers of Excellence and leading procurement operations.
Omar A. Tovar, Chief Logistics Officer, Urban Outfitters
Global retail and healthcare alignment
Omar oversees global supply chain operations for Urban Outfitters, Inc. (URBN), managing logistics, transportation, fulfilment innovation and systems integration across a global omni-channel network.
Supporting iconic brands like Anthropologie, Free People and Urban Outfitters, Omar has successfully expanded URBN's fulfilment footprint through advanced automation.
With more than 30 years of apparel manufacturing and supply chain experience across North America, Latin America, Asia and Europe, he previously held leadership roles at Nike and Coach.
The GS1 US Board of Governors comprises senior executives from leading organisations including Amazon, Coca-Cola, Dot Foods, Golden State Foods, Google, Johnson & Johnson, Kaiser Permanente, Kroger, PepsiCo, The Procter & Gamble Company, Publix Super Markets, PVH Americas & CK Global, Sysco, SYGMA and Guest Worldwide, Target, Topco Associates, Urban Outfitters, Wakefern Food Corp, Walmart and Wegmans Food Markets.

title: How NVIDIA and Corning are Creating US Supply Chains
content:
Article
Technology
How NVIDIA and Corning are Creating US Supply Chains
By
Louise Collins
May 26, 2026
undefined mins
Share
Share
Jensen Huang, Founder and CEO of NVIDIA
NVIDIA and Corning announced major US manufacturing partnership to produce optical connectivity components for data centres, creating more than 3,000 jobs
NVIDIA
and Corning have disclosed a multi-year agreement focused on optical connectivity manufacturing in the US.
The partnership could reshape domestic production capacity for data centre infrastructure components.
According to the announcement, the deal includes three new manufacturing sites expected to generate more than 3,000 jobs.
NVIDIA characterises the
technology as necessary
for what it terms next-generation AI infrastructure.
US manufacturing capacity expansion
Corning plans to increase its US optical connectivity manufacturing capacity by 10 times. The company also intends to expand its US fibre production capacity by more than 50%.
The buildout responds to accelerating demand from AI data centre construction. Corning attributes the expansion requirement to what it describes as AI factory deployments.
The three new facilities will be located in North Carolina and Texas. Corning states the expanded capacity will supply optical connectivity for hyperscale data centres deploying NVIDIA's
accelerated computing systems
.
According to Corning, the infrastructure supports thousands of NVIDIA GPUs that require high volumes of optical fibre and photonics to transfer data. NVIDIA says optical connectivity becomes a more important infrastructure component as AI facilities scale.
Supply chain localisation efforts
Jensen Huang, Founder and Chief Executive Officer of NVIDIA, says: "
AI is driving
the largest infrastructure buildout of our time and a once-in-a-generation opportunity to reinvigorate American manufacturing and supply chains.
Nvidia building. Credit: NVIDIA
"Together with Corning, we are inventing the future of computing with advanced optical technologies, building the foundation for AI infrastructure where intelligence moves at the speed of light while advancing the proud tradition of Made in America."
The partnership builds on Corning's existing US manufacturing expansion. In January 2026, Corning signed a multi-year agreement with Meta valued at US$6bn to supply optical fibre, cable and connectivity solutions.
Corning expanded manufacturing operations in North Carolina to support its Meta agreement. The NVIDIA partnership extends that earlier supply chain investment.
Financial and market response
According to The Wall Street Journal, NVIDIA is investing US$500m in Corning as part of the partnership. Corning's stock rose 17% following the announcement.
Corning developed low cost optical fibre in the 1970s. The company says its optical fibre products now face increased demand during the
AI data centre
construction cycle.
Wendell P Weeks, Chairman, Chief Executive Officer and President of Corning, says: "What NVIDIA is doing is nothing short of extraordinary, not just for the future of artificial intelligence, but for the American advanced manufacturing workforce.
Wendell P. Weeks, Chairman and CEO of Corning Incorporated. Credit: Corning
"Their commitment is directly fuelling the expansion of our US manufacturing footprint and creating more than 3,000 new, high-paying jobs for American workers."
"This partnership is proof that AI is not just a technology story. It is a manufacturing story and it is happening here in the US."
"Together with NVIDIA, we are ensuring the critical technologies
powering AI
are invented, engineered and built in America."
The agreement positions both companies to secure domestic supply chains for optical connectivity components. The expansion could reduce dependency on offshore manufacturing for data centre infrastructure materials.
